content.theory:
**Introduction to Python**

Python is one of the most popular programming languages in the world and is widely used in Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML), Web Development, Automation, Data Science, Cybersecurity, and many other fields.

Python was created by Guido van Rossum and released in 1991. It was designed to be simple, readable, and beginner-friendly. Unlike many programming languages that are difficult for beginners to understand, Python uses clean and easy-to-read syntax, making it one of the best languages for students and professionals starting their programming journey.

Today, companies like Google, Netflix, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and OpenAI use Python in their products and AI systems.

-----------------------------------

**Why Python is Important in AI and Machine Learning**

Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning require handling large amounts of data, mathematical computations, and model building. Python makes all of this easier because of its powerful libraries and simple syntax.

Python is the preferred language for AI because:

• It is beginner friendly
• It has simple and readable syntax
• It has a huge community support
• It provides thousands of libraries for AI and ML
• It supports automation and rapid development
• It works on Windows, Linux, and macOS

Popular Python libraries used in AI include:

• NumPy – Numerical computing
• Pandas – Data analysis
• Matplotlib – Data visualization
• Scikit-learn – Machine learning
• TensorFlow – Deep learning
• PyTorch – Neural networks
• OpenCV – Computer vision
• NLTK – Natural language processing

Python allows developers to focus more on solving problems instead of worrying about complex programming syntax.

-----------------------------------

**Applications of Python**

Python is used in many real-world applications.

Some common uses include:

1. Artificial Intelligence
Building AI chatbots, recommendation systems, and intelligent applications.

2. Machine Learning
Training systems to learn patterns from data.

3. Data Science
Analyzing and visualizing large datasets.

4. Web Development
Creating websites and backend applications using frameworks like Django and Flask.

5. Automation
Automating repetitive tasks such as file handling, emailing, and web scraping.

6. Cybersecurity
Building security tools, scanners, and automation scripts.

7. Game Development
Creating games using libraries like Pygame.

8. Cloud Computing
Managing cloud infrastructure and DevOps automation.

-----------------------------------

**Features of Python**

Python became extremely popular because of its powerful features.

1. Simple Syntax
Python code looks similar to English language sentences.

Example:

print("Hello World")

2. Easy to Learn
Beginners can understand Python quickly compared to languages like C++ or Java.

3. Open Source
Python is completely free to use.

4. Cross Platform
Python programs work on:

• Windows
• Linux
• macOS

5. Large Community Support
Millions of developers contribute tutorials, libraries, and solutions.

6. Extensive Libraries
Python provides ready-made libraries for almost every task.

-----------------------------------

**What is Python Interpreter?**

Python is an interpreted language.

This means:

• Python code is executed line by line
• No compilation is required before execution
• Errors can be detected quickly

The Python Interpreter reads and executes Python code.

When we write:

print("Hello")

The interpreter immediately executes the command and displays output.

-----------------------------------

**Installing Python**

Before writing Python programs, we must install Python on our system.

Steps to install Python:

1. Open the official website:
https://python.org/downloads

2. Download the latest stable version of Python.

3. Run the installer.

4. IMPORTANT:
Check the option:

✔ Add Python to PATH

This allows Python commands to work from the terminal.

5. Click Install.

6. After installation completes, verify installation.

-----------------------------------

**Verifying Python Installation**

Open Command Prompt or Terminal and type:

python --version

or

python3 --version

If Python is installed correctly, the version number will appear.

Example:

Python 3.12.0

-----------------------------------

**Installing VS Code**

VS Code (Visual Studio Code) is one of the most popular code editors used by developers.

It provides:

• Syntax highlighting
• Auto-completion
• Debugging
• Extensions
• Integrated terminal

Steps:

1. Download VS Code from:
https://code.visualstudio.com

2. Install VS Code.

3. Open VS Code.

4. Install the Python extension from Microsoft.

This extension enables:

• Python syntax support
• Running Python programs
• Debugging
• IntelliSense

-----------------------------------

**What is Jupyter Notebook?**

Jupyter Notebook is an interactive coding environment widely used in:

• AI
• Data Science
• Machine Learning
• Research

Jupyter allows users to:

• Write code
• Run code cell by cell
• Add notes and explanations
• Visualize graphs
• Test AI models interactively

Jupyter is extremely beginner-friendly.

-----------------------------------

**Installing Jupyter Notebook**

Open terminal or command prompt and type:

pip install notebook

After installation:

jupyter notebook

This opens Jupyter in your browser.

-----------------------------------

**What is pip?**

pip is Python’s package manager.

It allows us to install external libraries.

Example:

pip install numpy

This installs the NumPy library.

Without pip, manually managing libraries would be difficult.

-----------------------------------

**Running Python Programs**

Python programs can be executed in multiple ways.

1. Python REPL

REPL stands for:

• Read
• Evaluate
• Print
• Loop

Open terminal and type:

python

You can now execute Python commands interactively.

Example:

print("Hello")

2. Running Python File

Create a file named:

hello.py

Add:

print("Hello World")

Run using:

python hello.py

-----------------------------------

**Your First Python Program**

Example:

print("Welcome to AI Internship")

Explanation:

• print() is a built-in Python function
• Text inside quotes is called a string
• Python displays the text on screen

Output:

Welcome to AI Internship

-----------------------------------

**Comments in Python**

Comments are notes written inside code for better understanding.

Single-line comment:

# This is a comment

Comments are ignored by Python.

They help developers:

• Explain code
• Improve readability
• Document logic

-----------------------------------

**Why Learning Python Properly Matters**

Python is the foundation of your AI journey.

Almost every AI concept later in the internship depends on Python programming.

If your Python fundamentals are strong:

• Machine Learning becomes easier
• Deep Learning becomes easier
• Automation becomes easier
• Debugging becomes easier
• Building projects becomes easier

Strong basics create strong developers.

-----------------------------------

**Real-World AI Applications Built Using Python**

Python powers many AI systems we use daily.

Examples:

• ChatGPT
• YouTube recommendations
• Netflix recommendations
• Self-driving cars
• Face recognition
• AI voice assistants
• Medical diagnosis systems
• Fraud detection systems

Python is one of the most important skills in modern technology careers.

-----------------------------------

**Summary**

In this session, you learned:

• What Python is
• Why Python is used in AI
• Features of Python
• Applications of Python
• Installing Python
• Installing VS Code
• Introduction to Jupyter Notebook
• Running Python programs
• Python REPL basics
• Your first Python program

This is the first step toward becoming an AI Engineer, Machine Learning Engineer, Data Scientist, or AI Researcher.

The next sessions will build upon these fundamentals and gradually move toward advanced AI development.
keyPoints: Python is one of the most popular programming languages for AI and Machine Learning
Python is beginner friendly because of its simple syntax
Python is used in AI, Data Science, Automation, Web Development, and Cybersecurity
Popular AI libraries include NumPy, Pandas, TensorFlow, and PyTorch
Python is an interpreted programming language
VS Code is a popular code editor for Python development
Jupyter Notebook is widely used for AI and Data Science projects
pip is used to install Python libraries and packages
Python programs can run using REPL or Python script files
print() is used to display output in Python
Comments improve code readability and documentation
Strong Python fundamentals are essential for learning AI and Machine Learning
content.practicalTask: Install Python and VS Code. Install the Python extension in VS Code. Create a file named hello.py and print 'Welcome to AI Internship'. Open Python REPL and perform 5 simple print operations.
content.assignment: Set up the development environment successfully. Create a Python file that prints your name, college, favorite technology, and a motivational message using separate print() statements.
content.miniProject: Build a simple 'Student Introduction Program' that displays student details using multiple print statements and comments.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Variables and Data Types**

In programming, data is one of the most important things we work with.

Applications store:

• User names
• Passwords
• Numbers
• Images
• AI predictions
• Sensor readings
• Messages

To work with data, programming languages use:

• Variables
• Data Types
• Operators

These are the building blocks of every Python program.

Understanding them properly is extremely important because:

• AI models work with data
• Machine Learning uses numbers and calculations
• Python programs depend heavily on variables and operators

Strong understanding of variables and data types helps students write better programs and understand advanced AI concepts later.

-----------------------------------

**What is a Variable?**

A variable is a container used to store data.

Think of a variable like a labeled box.

Example:

name = "Pravesh"

Here:

• name → variable name
• "Pravesh" → stored value

The variable stores information so that we can use it later in the program.

-----------------------------------

**Why Variables are Important**

Variables allow programs to:

• Store user input
• Perform calculations
• Save temporary results
• Process AI data
• Reuse information

Without variables, programming would become extremely difficult.

-----------------------------------

**Rules for Naming Variables**

Python follows specific rules for variable names.

-----------------------------------

**Valid Variable Names**

Example:

age = 20

student_name = "Rahul"

marks1 = 95

-----------------------------------

**Invalid Variable Names**

Example:

1name = "John"

student-name = "Rahul"

class = 10

Why invalid?

• Cannot start with numbers
• Cannot contain special symbols like -
• Cannot use Python keywords

-----------------------------------

**Best Practices for Variable Names**

Use meaningful names.

Bad Example:

x = 95

Good Example:

student_marks = 95

Meaningful variable names improve:

• Readability
• Debugging
• Collaboration

-----------------------------------

**What are Data Types?**

Data types define:

What kind of data a variable stores.

Different types of data require different operations.

Example:

• Numbers can be added
• Text can be joined
• Boolean values represent True or False

-----------------------------------

**Major Python Data Types**

Python provides several built-in data types.

The most important beginner data types are:

1. Integer (int)
2. Float (float)
3. String (str)
4. Boolean (bool)

-----------------------------------

**Integer (int)**

Integers are whole numbers.

Examples:

age = 20

year = 2026

marks = 95

Integers do not contain decimal values.

-----------------------------------

**Float (float)**

Floats are decimal numbers.

Examples:

height = 5.8

price = 99.99

temperature = 36.5

Floats are heavily used in:

• AI calculations
• Machine Learning
• Statistics
• Scientific computing

-----------------------------------

**String (str)**

Strings represent text.

Strings are written inside:

• Single quotes ''
• Double quotes ""

Examples:

name = "Pravesh"

college = 'Presidency University'

-----------------------------------

**String Operations**

Strings can be combined.

Example:

first_name = "Pravesh"

last_name = "Dubey"

full_name = first_name + " " + last_name

Output:

Pravesh Dubey

This process is called:

String concatenation.

-----------------------------------

**Boolean (bool)**

Boolean values represent:

• True
• False

Examples:

is_logged_in = True

is_admin = False

Booleans are very important in:

• Decision making
• AI logic
• Conditions
• Authentication systems

-----------------------------------

**Checking Data Types**

Python provides the type() function.

Example:

age = 20

print(type(age))

Output:

<class 'int'>

This helps programmers identify data types.

-----------------------------------

**Dynamic Typing in Python**

Python is dynamically typed.

This means:

We do not need to specify the data type manually.

Example:

name = "Pravesh"

age = 20

Python automatically understands:

• name is string
• age is integer

This makes Python beginner friendly.

-----------------------------------

**Type Conversion (Type Casting)**

Sometimes we need to convert one data type into another.

This process is called:

Type Casting.

-----------------------------------

**Converting Integer to Float**

Example:

num = 10

result = float(num)

print(result)

Output:

10.0

-----------------------------------

**Converting Float to Integer**

Example:

price = 99.99

new_price = int(price)

print(new_price)

Output:

99

Decimal part gets removed.

-----------------------------------

**Converting String to Integer**

Example:

age = "20"

new_age = int(age)

print(new_age)

This is important because:

User input is received as string by default.

-----------------------------------

**What are Operators?**

Operators perform operations on values and variables.

Example:

5 + 3

Here:

+ is an operator.

-----------------------------------

**Arithmetic Operators**

Used for mathematical calculations.

-----------------------------------

**Addition (+)**

Example:

print(10 + 5)

Output:

15

-----------------------------------

**Subtraction (-)**

Example:

print(10 - 5)

Output:

5

-----------------------------------

**Multiplication (*)**

Example:

print(10 * 5)

Output:

50

-----------------------------------

**Division (/)**

Example:

print(10 / 2)

Output:

5.0

-----------------------------------

**Modulus (%)**

Returns remainder.

Example:

print(10 % 3)

Output:

1

-----------------------------------

**Exponent (**)**

Used for powers.

Example:

print(2 ** 3)

Output:

8

-----------------------------------

**Floor Division (//)**

Removes decimal part.

Example:

print(10 // 3)

Output:

3

-----------------------------------

**Comparison Operators**

Comparison operators compare values.

They return:

• True
• False

-----------------------------------

**Equal To (==)**

Example:

print(10 == 10)

Output:

True

-----------------------------------

**Not Equal To (!=)**

Example:

print(10 != 5)

Output:

True

-----------------------------------

**Greater Than (>)**

Example:

print(20 > 10)

Output:

True

-----------------------------------

**Less Than (<)**

Example:

print(5 < 2)

Output:

False

-----------------------------------

**Greater Than or Equal To (>=)**

Example:

print(10 >= 10)

Output:

True

-----------------------------------

**Less Than or Equal To (<=)**

Example:

print(5 <= 10)

Output:

True

-----------------------------------

**Operator Precedence**

Python follows mathematical precedence rules.

Example:

print(10 + 5 * 2)

Output:

20

Why?

Multiplication happens before addition.

To change priority:

Use brackets.

Example:

print((10 + 5) * 2)

Output:

30

-----------------------------------

**User Input in Python**

Python uses input() to take user input.

Example:

name = input("Enter your name: ")

print(name)

Important:

input() returns string data.

If numerical calculations are required:

Convert input using int() or float().

-----------------------------------

**BMI Calculator Logic**

BMI stands for:

Body Mass Index.

Formula:

BMI = weight / (height * height)

Example:

weight = 70

height = 1.75

bmi = weight / (height ** 2)

print(bmi)

This combines:

• Variables
• Floats
• Arithmetic operators

-----------------------------------

**String Formatting**

String formatting creates readable output.

Example:

name = "Pravesh"

age = 20

print(f"My name is {name} and I am {age} years old")

This is called:

f-string formatting.

It is modern and beginner friendly.

-----------------------------------

**Common Beginner Mistakes**

1. Forgetting quotes around strings

Wrong:

name = Pravesh

Correct:

name = "Pravesh"

2. Mixing strings and integers

Wrong:

age = 20

print("Age is " + age)

Correct:

print("Age is", age)

or

print(f"Age is {age}")

3. Using = instead of ==

= means assignment

== means comparison

-----------------------------------

**Real-World Importance in AI**

Variables and data types are used everywhere in AI.

Examples:

• Storing datasets
• Model predictions
• Accuracy values
• User input
• Training results
• Neural network weights

Operators are used in:

• Mathematical calculations
• AI algorithms
• Data analysis
• Machine Learning models

Without understanding variables and operators, AI programming becomes impossible.

-----------------------------------

**Summary**

In this session, you learned:

• What variables are
• Rules for variable naming
• Python data types
• Integers, floats, strings, booleans
• Dynamic typing
• Type casting
• Arithmetic operators
• Comparison operators
• Operator precedence
• User input handling
• String formatting
• BMI calculation logic

These concepts form the foundation of all future Python and AI programming concepts.
keyPoints: Variables are used to store data in Python
Python supports integers, floats, strings, and booleans
Python is dynamically typed
type() is used to identify data types
Type casting converts one data type into another
Arithmetic operators perform mathematical calculations
Comparison operators return True or False
Operator precedence controls execution order
input() is used to accept user input
f-strings provide readable string formatting
Variables and operators are essential in AI and Machine Learning
Proper understanding of data types helps in building efficient AI applications
content.practicalTask: Create a BMI Calculator using variables, float values, arithmetic operators, and user input. Display the final BMI using formatted output.
content.assignment: Write a Python program that accepts two numbers from the user and performs addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, modulus, and exponent operations.
content.miniProject: Build a Simple Student Information System that stores student name, age, marks, and calculates average marks using operators.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Control Flow**

In programming, computers execute code line by line.

However, real-world applications often need to:

• Make decisions
• Repeat tasks
• Execute logic based on conditions
• Stop execution under certain situations

This is where:

Control Flow

becomes important.

Control flow determines:

How a program moves from one instruction to another.

Without control flow, programs would only execute instructions sequentially and would not be intelligent.

Control flow allows programs to:

• Take decisions
• Perform repetitive tasks
• Automate workflows
• Build AI logic
• Process large datasets

Control flow is one of the most important foundations in Python programming and Artificial Intelligence.

-----------------------------------

**Why Control Flow is Important in AI**

Artificial Intelligence systems constantly make decisions.

Examples:

• Spam detection systems decide whether an email is spam or not
• Recommendation systems decide which movie to suggest
• Self-driving cars decide when to stop or move
• Chatbots decide how to respond
• Face recognition systems decide if a face matches

These decisions are possible because of:

• Conditions
• Loops
• Logic

Understanding control flow is critical before learning Machine Learning and AI algorithms.

-----------------------------------

**What are Conditions?**

Conditions allow programs to make decisions.

A condition checks whether something is:

• True
• False

Based on the result, the program executes different code.

-----------------------------------

**The if Statement**

The if statement executes code only if the condition is true.

Example:

age = 18

if age >= 18:
    print("You are eligible to vote")

Explanation:

• age >= 18 is the condition
• If the condition is True, the print statement executes

-----------------------------------

**Importance of Indentation in Python**

Python uses indentation to define blocks of code.

Example:

if True:
    print("Hello")

The indented line belongs to the if block.

Without proper indentation:

Python gives errors.

Indentation is mandatory in Python.

-----------------------------------

**The else Statement**

The else block executes when the condition is false.

Example:

age = 15

if age >= 18:
    print("Eligible")
else:
    print("Not Eligible")

Output:

Not Eligible

-----------------------------------

**The elif Statement**

elif means:

Else If.

Used when multiple conditions need to be checked.

Example:

marks = 85

if marks >= 90:
    print("Grade A")
elif marks >= 75:
    print("Grade B")
else:
    print("Grade C")

Output:

Grade B

-----------------------------------

**Flow of Conditions**

Python checks conditions from top to bottom.

Once a condition becomes True:

Remaining conditions are skipped.

-----------------------------------

**Boolean Expressions**

Conditions use Boolean expressions.

Boolean expressions return:

• True
• False

Examples:

10 > 5

20 == 10

5 != 3

-----------------------------------

**Logical Operators**

Logical operators combine multiple conditions.

-----------------------------------

**AND Operator**

Returns True only if both conditions are true.

Example:

age = 20

citizen = True

if age >= 18 and citizen:
    print("Eligible to vote")

-----------------------------------

**OR Operator**

Returns True if at least one condition is true.

Example:

if age >= 18 or citizen:
    print("Condition met")

-----------------------------------

**NOT Operator**

Reverses the condition.

Example:

is_logged_in = False

if not is_logged_in:
    print("Please Login")

-----------------------------------

**What are Loops?**

Loops are used to repeat code multiple times.

Without loops:

Developers would have to write repetitive code manually.

Loops make programs:

• Faster
• Cleaner
• More efficient

-----------------------------------

**Why Loops are Important in AI**

AI systems process huge amounts of data.

Loops help:

• Process datasets
• Train models repeatedly
• Analyze thousands of records
• Automate repetitive tasks

Loops are heavily used in:

• Machine Learning
• Data Analysis
• Neural Networks
• Deep Learning

-----------------------------------

**The for Loop**

The for loop is used for iteration.

Iteration means:

Repeating through items one by one.

Example:

for i in range(5):
    print(i)

Output:

0
1
2
3
4

-----------------------------------

**Understanding range()**

range() generates numbers.

Example:

range(5)

Generates:

0, 1, 2, 3, 4

-----------------------------------

**Loop Variable**

In:

for i in range(5)

• i is the loop variable
• It changes automatically during iteration

-----------------------------------

**Custom Range**

Example:

for i in range(1, 6):
    print(i)

Output:

1
2
3
4
5

-----------------------------------

**Step Value in range()**

Example:

for i in range(0, 10, 2):
    print(i)

Output:

0
2
4
6
8

The third value defines:

Step size.

-----------------------------------

**The while Loop**

while loops execute while a condition remains true.

Example:

count = 1

while count <= 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1

Output:

1
2
3
4
5

-----------------------------------

**Infinite Loops**

If the condition never becomes false:

The loop runs forever.

Example:

while True:
    print("Hello")

This creates an infinite loop.

Infinite loops can crash programs if not handled carefully.

-----------------------------------

**Loop Control Statements**

Python provides special statements to control loops.

-----------------------------------

**break Statement**

break stops the loop immediately.

Example:

for i in range(10):
    if i == 5:
        break
    print(i)

Output:

0
1
2
3
4

Loop stops when i becomes 5.

-----------------------------------

**continue Statement**

continue skips the current iteration.

Example:

for i in range(5):
    if i == 2:
        continue
    print(i)

Output:

0
1
3
4

The value 2 is skipped.

-----------------------------------

**Nested Loops**

A loop inside another loop is called a nested loop.

Example:

for i in range(3):
    for j in range(2):
        print(i, j)

Output:

0 0
0 1
1 0
1 1
2 0
2 1

-----------------------------------

**Why Nested Loops are Important**

Nested loops are used in:

• Matrix operations
• AI data processing
• Image processing
• Pattern generation
• Neural network computations

-----------------------------------

**Multiplication Table Example**

Example:

num = 5

for i in range(1, 11):
    print(num, "x", i, "=", num * i)

Output:

5 x 1 = 5
5 x 2 = 10
...

This combines:

• Variables
• Loops
• Arithmetic operators

-----------------------------------

**Number Guessing Game Logic**

A guessing game uses:

• Conditions
• Loops
• User input

Basic Logic:

1. Store a secret number
2. Ask user for input repeatedly
3. Compare input with secret number
4. Stop loop if guessed correctly

-----------------------------------

**Common Beginner Mistakes**

1. Forgetting indentation

Wrong indentation causes syntax errors.

2. Infinite loops

Always update loop conditions.

3. Using = instead of ==

= means assignment

== means comparison

4. Incorrect loop range

range(5) ends at 4, not 5.

-----------------------------------

**Real-World Applications of Loops and Conditions**

Conditions and loops are everywhere in technology.

Examples:

• ATM systems
• Login systems
• Chatbots
• Recommendation systems
• AI decision systems
• Mobile applications
• Web applications
• Data processing pipelines

-----------------------------------

**Control Flow in AI Systems**

AI systems use control flow constantly.

Examples:

• If confidence score > 90%, approve prediction
• Repeat training loop for 100 epochs
• Continue training until loss decreases
• Stop processing if error occurs

Machine Learning algorithms rely heavily on:

• Loops
• Conditions
• Boolean logic

-----------------------------------

**Summary**

In this session, you learned:

• What control flow is
• Why conditions are important
• if, elif, else statements
• Boolean expressions
• Logical operators
• for loops
• while loops
• break and continue
• Nested loops
• Multiplication table logic
• Number guessing game logic
• Real-world AI applications of loops and conditions

These concepts form the foundation for writing intelligent programs and AI systems.
keyPoints: Control flow determines how programs execute instructions
Conditions help programs make decisions
if, elif, and else are used for conditional execution
Boolean expressions return True or False
Logical operators combine multiple conditions
Loops repeat code multiple times
for loops are commonly used for iteration
while loops execute while conditions remain true
break stops loops immediately
continue skips the current iteration
Nested loops are loops inside other loops
Control flow concepts are heavily used in AI and Machine Learning
content.practicalTask: Write a Python program that prints multiplication tables from 1 to 10 using nested loops.
content.assignment: Build a Number Guessing Game where the program stores a secret number and repeatedly asks the user to guess until the correct number is entered.
content.miniProject: Build a Simple Student Grade Analyzer that accepts marks from the user, checks grades using conditions, and displays class rankings using loops.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Functions in Python**

As programs become larger, writing all code in one place becomes difficult.

Problems with large unstructured programs:

• Difficult to understand
• Difficult to debug
• Repetitive code
• Hard to maintain
• Hard to reuse logic

To solve this problem, programming languages use:

Functions.

Functions are one of the most important concepts in programming.

They help developers:

• Organize code
• Reuse logic
• Reduce repetition
• Build modular applications
• Write cleaner programs

Functions are heavily used in:

• Artificial Intelligence
• Machine Learning
• Web Development
• Data Science
• Automation

Understanding functions properly is essential for becoming a good programmer.

-----------------------------------

**What is a Function?**

A function is a reusable block of code designed to perform a specific task.

Think of a function like a machine.

Input goes inside → processing happens → output comes out.

Example:

A calculator addition feature:

• Input → two numbers
• Process → add them
• Output → result

Instead of rewriting the same logic again and again, we create a function.

-----------------------------------

**Why Functions are Important**

Functions provide many advantages.

-----------------------------------

**1. Code Reusability**

Write once → use multiple times.

Example:

A login validation function can be reused throughout the application.

-----------------------------------

**2. Better Readability**

Functions divide programs into smaller understandable parts.

Instead of reading 500 lines together:

Programs become modular.

-----------------------------------

**3. Easier Debugging**

Errors can be identified inside specific functions.

-----------------------------------

**4. Faster Development**

Developers can reuse already written functions.

-----------------------------------

**5. Important in AI**

AI applications contain:

• Data preprocessing functions
• Model training functions
• Prediction functions
• Evaluation functions

Functions are everywhere in AI programming.

-----------------------------------

**Defining a Function**

Python uses the def keyword to create functions.

Basic Syntax:

Example:


def greet():
    print("Hello")

Explanation:

• def → keyword used to define functions
• greet → function name
• () → parentheses
• print() → code inside the function

-----------------------------------

**Calling a Function**

Defining a function does not execute it.

To run the function:

We must call it.

Example:


def greet():
    print("Hello")

greet()

Output:

Hello

-----------------------------------

**Function Execution Flow**

When a function is called:

1. Program jumps into the function
2. Executes all code inside
3. Returns back to original location

-----------------------------------

**Functions with Parameters**

Sometimes functions need input values.

These inputs are called:

Parameters.

Example:


def greet(name):
    print("Hello", name)

greet("Pravesh")

Output:

Hello Pravesh

-----------------------------------

**What are Parameters?**

Parameters are variables defined inside the function declaration.

Example:


def add(a, b):
    print(a + b)

Here:

• a and b are parameters

-----------------------------------

**What are Arguments?**

Arguments are actual values passed to the function.

Example:

add(10, 20)

Here:

• 10 and 20 are arguments

-----------------------------------

**Arguments vs Parameters**

Parameters:

Variables inside function definition.

Arguments:

Actual values sent during function call.

-----------------------------------

**Functions with Multiple Parameters**

Example:


def student(name, age):
    print(name)
    print(age)

student("Rahul", 20)

-----------------------------------

**Return Values**

Functions can send data back using:

return.

Example:


def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(10, 5)

print(result)

Output:

15

-----------------------------------

**Why return is Important**

Without return:

Functions only display output.

With return:

Functions can send results for further processing.

AI systems heavily depend on return values.

Example:

• Prediction results
• Accuracy scores
• Data outputs
• Model evaluation

-----------------------------------

**Difference Between print() and return**

print():

Displays output.

return:

Sends output back to the program.

Example:


def test():
    print("Hello")

value = test()

print(value)

Output:

Hello
None

Why None?

Because the function did not return anything.

-----------------------------------

**Default Arguments**

Functions can have default values.

Example:


def greet(name="Guest"):
    print("Hello", name)

greet()

greet("Pravesh")

Output:

Hello Guest
Hello Pravesh

-----------------------------------

**Why Default Arguments are Useful**

They make functions:

• Flexible
• Easier to use
• More professional

Many Python libraries use default arguments.

-----------------------------------

**Keyword Arguments**

Python allows sending arguments using parameter names.

Example:


def student(name, age):
    print(name)
    print(age)

student(age=20, name="Rahul")

Output:

Rahul
20

-----------------------------------

**Variable Scope**

Scope defines where variables can be accessed.

Python mainly has:

• Local scope
• Global scope

-----------------------------------

**Local Variables**

Variables created inside functions are called local variables.

Example:


def test():
    x = 10
    print(x)

test()

print(x)

This causes error outside the function.

Why?

Because x only exists inside the function.

-----------------------------------

**Why Local Variables are Important**

They:

• Prevent conflicts
• Protect data
• Improve security
• Keep programs organized

-----------------------------------

**Global Variables**

Variables created outside functions are global variables.

Example:

name = "Pravesh"


def display():
    print(name)

display()

Output:

Pravesh

Global variables can be accessed throughout the program.

-----------------------------------

**Modifying Global Variables**

Python requires global keyword.

Example:

count = 0


def increase():
    global count
    count += 1

increase()

print(count)

Output:

1

-----------------------------------

**Why Excessive Global Variables are Dangerous**

Too many global variables create:

• Security risks
• Debugging difficulties
• Unpredictable behavior

Professional developers prefer local variables whenever possible.

-----------------------------------

**Nested Functions**

Functions can exist inside other functions.

Example:


def outer():
    def inner():
        print("Inside Inner Function")

    inner()

outer()

-----------------------------------

**Recursion**

A function calling itself is called recursion.

Example:


def countdown(n):
    if n == 0:
        return

    print(n)
    countdown(n - 1)

countdown(5)

-----------------------------------

**Factorial Example Using Functions**

Factorial means:

5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1

Example:


def factorial(n):
    result = 1

    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        result *= i

    return result

print(factorial(5))

Output:

120

-----------------------------------

**Palindrome Checker Function**

Palindrome:

A word that reads the same forward and backward.

Examples:

• madam
• level
• radar

Example:


def palindrome(word):
    if word == word[::-1]:
        return True
    return False

-----------------------------------

**Area Calculator Function**

Example:


def area(length, width):
    return length * width

print(area(5, 10))

Output:

50

-----------------------------------

**Functions in AI and Machine Learning**

Functions are everywhere in AI.

Examples:

• train_model()
• predict_output()
• preprocess_data()
• evaluate_model()
• calculate_accuracy()

Large AI systems contain thousands of functions.

Without functions:

AI development becomes impossible.

-----------------------------------

**Real-World Applications of Functions**

Functions are used in:

• Banking software
• Mobile apps
• Websites
• AI systems
• Games
• Robotics
• Automation scripts
• Chatbots

-----------------------------------

**Common Beginner Mistakes**

1. Forgetting to call functions

Defining is not enough.

2. Missing return statements

Without return, functions return None.

3. Incorrect indentation

Python depends heavily on indentation.

4. Using global variables excessively

Can create difficult bugs.

5. Confusing arguments and parameters

Parameters = definition

Arguments = actual values

-----------------------------------

**Best Practices for Writing Functions**

1. Use meaningful names

Good Example:

calculate_bmi()

Bad Example:

func1()

2. Keep functions small

Each function should perform one task.

3. Use comments when necessary

4. Avoid unnecessary global variables

5. Reuse functions instead of rewriting code

-----------------------------------

**Summary**

In this session, you learned:

• What functions are
• Why functions are important
• Defining functions using def
• Calling functions
• Parameters and arguments
• Return values
• Default arguments
• Local and global variables
• Variable scope
• Recursive functions
• Real-world examples like factorial and palindrome checker
• Importance of functions in AI and software development

Functions are one of the strongest foundations in programming and are used in almost every AI and Machine Learning application.
keyPoints: Functions are reusable blocks of code
Python uses the def keyword to create functions
Parameters receive values inside functions
Arguments are actual values passed during function calls
return sends values back from functions
Default arguments make functions flexible
Local variables exist only inside functions
Global variables can be accessed throughout the program
Functions improve code readability and reusability
Functions are heavily used in AI and Machine Learning
Recursive functions call themselves
Proper function design improves software quality

content.assignment: Build a Grade Calculator using functions that accepts marks of multiple subjects and returns total marks, average percentage, and final grade.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Python Collections**

In programming, applications often need to store multiple values together.

Examples:

• Student names
• AI datasets
• Product prices
• User details
• Sensor readings
• Chat messages
• Machine Learning training data

Storing each value in a separate variable becomes difficult.

Example:

student1 = "Rahul"
student2 = "Aman"
student3 = "Priya"

This approach is inefficient.

To solve this problem, Python provides:

Collections.

Collections are special data structures used to store multiple values efficiently.

Python provides four major built-in collections:

1. Lists
2. Tuples
3. Dictionaries
4. Sets

These are among the most important concepts in Python programming and Artificial Intelligence.

-----------------------------------

**Why Collections are Important in AI and Machine Learning**

AI systems process huge amounts of data.

Examples:

• Thousands of images
• Millions of text records
• Sensor data
• User activity logs
• Neural network weights
• Predictions and outputs

Collections help organize and manage this data efficiently.

Without collections:

Building AI systems becomes almost impossible.

-----------------------------------

# LISTS

-----------------------------------

**What is a List?**

A list is an ordered collection used to store multiple values.

Lists are one of the most commonly used Python data structures.

Example:

students = ["Rahul", "Priya", "Aman"]

-----------------------------------

**Characteristics of Lists**

Lists are:

• Ordered
• Mutable
• Allow duplicate values
• Store multiple data types

-----------------------------------

**What Does Ordered Mean?**

Ordered means:

Items maintain their position.

Example:

numbers = [10, 20, 30]

• 10 is at index 0
• 20 is at index 1
• 30 is at index 2

-----------------------------------

**What Does Mutable Mean?**

Mutable means:

Data can be changed after creation.

Example:

fruits = ["Apple", "Banana"]

fruits[0] = "Mango"

print(fruits)

Output:

['Mango', 'Banana']

-----------------------------------

**Accessing List Elements**

Lists use indexing.

Example:

numbers = [10, 20, 30]

print(numbers[0])

Output:

10

-----------------------------------

**Negative Indexing**

Python supports negative indexing.

Example:

numbers = [10, 20, 30]

print(numbers[-1])

Output:

30

-1 refers to the last element.

-----------------------------------

**Adding Elements to Lists**

Python provides multiple methods.

-----------------------------------

**append()**

Adds item at the end.

Example:

numbers = [1, 2]

numbers.append(3)

print(numbers)

Output:

[1, 2, 3]

-----------------------------------

**insert()**

Adds item at specific position.

Example:

numbers.insert(1, 100)

-----------------------------------

**extend()**

Adds multiple items.

Example:

numbers.extend([4, 5])

-----------------------------------

**Removing Elements from Lists**

-----------------------------------

**remove()**

Removes specific value.

Example:

numbers.remove(2)

-----------------------------------

**pop()**

Removes element using index.

Example:

numbers.pop(0)

-----------------------------------

**clear()**

Removes all elements.

Example:

numbers.clear()

-----------------------------------

**List Length**

Use len().

Example:

students = ["A", "B", "C"]

print(len(students))

Output:

3

-----------------------------------

**Looping Through Lists**

Example:

students = ["Rahul", "Priya", "Aman"]

for student in students:
    print(student)

-----------------------------------

**List Slicing**

Slicing extracts parts of lists.

Example:

numbers = [1,2,3,4,5]

print(numbers[1:4])

Output:

[2,3,4]

-----------------------------------

**Nested Lists**

Lists inside lists are called nested lists.

Example:

matrix = [[1,2],[3,4]]

Nested lists are heavily used in:

• AI datasets
• Matrix operations
• Deep Learning
• Image processing

-----------------------------------

# TUPLES

-----------------------------------

**What is a Tuple?**

A tuple is similar to a list but immutable.

Example:

data = (10, 20, 30)

-----------------------------------

**Characteristics of Tuples**

Tuples are:

• Ordered
• Immutable
• Allow duplicates

-----------------------------------

**What Does Immutable Mean?**

Immutable means:

Data cannot be changed after creation.

Example:

numbers = (1,2,3)

numbers[0] = 100

This causes an error.

-----------------------------------

**Why Tuples are Important**

Tuples provide:

• Better security
• Faster performance
• Data protection

Used when data should not change.

-----------------------------------

**Tuple Packing and Unpacking**

Example:

person = ("Rahul", 20)

name, age = person

print(name)
print(age)

-----------------------------------

# DICTIONARIES

-----------------------------------

**What is a Dictionary?**

A dictionary stores data using:

Key → Value pairs.

Example:

student = {
    "name": "Rahul",
    "age": 20,
    "marks": 95
}

-----------------------------------

**Why Dictionaries are Powerful**

Dictionaries allow fast data retrieval.

Instead of searching by position:

We search using keys.

-----------------------------------

**Accessing Dictionary Values**

Example:

print(student["name"])

Output:

Rahul

-----------------------------------

**Adding New Values**

Example:

student["college"] = "Presidency University"

-----------------------------------

**Updating Dictionary Values**

Example:

student["marks"] = 99

-----------------------------------

**Removing Values**

Example:

student.pop("age")

-----------------------------------

**Dictionary Methods**

-----------------------------------

**keys()**

Returns all keys.

Example:

print(student.keys())

-----------------------------------

**values()**

Returns all values.

Example:

print(student.values())

-----------------------------------

**items()**

Returns both keys and values.

Example:

print(student.items())

-----------------------------------

**Looping Through Dictionaries**

Example:

for key, value in student.items():
    print(key, value)

-----------------------------------

**Real-World Usage of Dictionaries**

Dictionaries are widely used in:

• APIs
• JSON data
• AI models
• Databases
• User profiles
• Chatbots

-----------------------------------

# SETS

-----------------------------------

**What is a Set?**

A set is an unordered collection of unique values.

Example:

numbers = {1,2,3,4}

-----------------------------------

**Characteristics of Sets**

Sets:

• Do not allow duplicates
• Are unordered
• Are mutable

-----------------------------------

**Duplicate Removal**

Example:

numbers = {1,1,2,3}

print(numbers)

Output:

{1,2,3}

-----------------------------------

**Adding Values to Sets**

Example:

numbers.add(5)

-----------------------------------

**Removing Values from Sets**

Example:

numbers.remove(2)

-----------------------------------

**Set Operations**

-----------------------------------

**Union**

Combines sets.

Example:

A = {1,2,3}
B = {3,4,5}

print(A | B)

Output:

{1,2,3,4,5}

-----------------------------------

**Intersection**

Common elements.

Example:

print(A & B)

Output:

{3}

-----------------------------------

**Difference**

Unique elements.

Example:

print(A - B)

Output:

{1,2}

-----------------------------------

**Why Sets are Important in AI**

Sets are useful for:

• Removing duplicate data
• Data preprocessing
• NLP word analysis
• Recommendation systems

-----------------------------------

# LIST COMPREHENSIONS

-----------------------------------

**What is List Comprehension?**

List comprehension provides a shorter way to create lists.

Example:

numbers = [x for x in range(5)]

Output:

[0,1,2,3,4]

-----------------------------------

**Traditional vs List Comprehension**

Traditional:

numbers = []

for i in range(5):
    numbers.append(i)

List Comprehension:

numbers = [i for i in range(5)]

-----------------------------------

**List Comprehension with Conditions**

Example:

even = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]

Output:

[0,2,4,6,8]

-----------------------------------

**Why List Comprehensions are Important**

They:

• Reduce code size
• Improve readability
• Improve performance

Widely used in:

• AI preprocessing
• Data analysis
• Machine Learning pipelines

-----------------------------------

# MUTABLE VS IMMUTABLE

-----------------------------------

**Mutable Data Types**

Can be changed.

Examples:

• Lists
• Dictionaries
• Sets

-----------------------------------

**Immutable Data Types**

Cannot be changed.

Examples:

• Tuples
• Strings
• Integers

-----------------------------------

# COLLECTIONS IN AI AND MACHINE LEARNING

-----------------------------------

Collections are heavily used in AI.

Examples:

Lists:

• Store datasets
• Store predictions

Dictionaries:

• Store labeled data
• JSON APIs

Sets:

• Remove duplicate records

Tuples:

• Fixed AI configurations

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

-----------------------------------

**Student Management System**

Uses:

• Lists for students
• Dictionaries for profiles
• Sets for unique courses

-----------------------------------

**Chatbot Systems**

Use:

• Dictionaries for intents
• Lists for messages
• Sets for unique keywords

-----------------------------------

**Recommendation Systems**

Use:

• Lists for user history
• Sets for common interests
• Dictionaries for user profiles

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Confusing lists and tuples

Lists are mutable.

Tuples are immutable.

-----------------------------------

2. Using wrong dictionary keys

Accessing non-existing keys causes errors.

-----------------------------------

3. Forgetting sets remove duplicates

-----------------------------------

4. Incorrect indexing

Python indexing starts from 0.

-----------------------------------

5. Modifying tuples

Tuples cannot be changed.

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Use lists for changeable data

2. Use tuples for fixed data

3. Use dictionaries for structured information

4. Use sets for unique values

5. Use meaningful variable names

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What collections are
• Lists and list methods
• Tuples and immutability
• Dictionaries and key-value pairs
• Sets and unique values
• List comprehensions
• Mutable vs immutable data types
• Real-world AI applications of collections

Collections are one of the most powerful features of Python and are used extensively in Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Data Science, APIs, and modern software development.
keyPoints: Lists are ordered and mutable collections
Tuples are ordered but immutable
Dictionaries store data as key-value pairs
Sets store unique unordered values
List methods include append(), insert(), remove(), and pop()
Dictionaries provide fast data retrieval using keys
Sets automatically remove duplicate values
List comprehensions create lists efficiently
Mutable objects can be modified after creation
Immutable objects cannot be modified
Collections are heavily used in AI and Machine Learning
Proper use of collections improves program efficiency

content.theory:
**Introduction to Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**

As software applications become larger and more complex, organizing code properly becomes extremely important.

Imagine building:

• A banking application
• An AI chatbot
• A hospital management system
• A social media platform
• A Machine Learning system

If all logic is written in one place:

• Code becomes messy
• Maintenance becomes difficult
• Debugging becomes harder
• Reusability decreases

To solve these problems, modern programming uses:

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP).

OOP is one of the most powerful programming paradigms used in real-world software development.

Python fully supports Object-Oriented Programming.

-----------------------------------

**What is Object-Oriented Programming?**

Object-Oriented Programming is a programming approach where programs are designed using:

• Objects
• Classes

Instead of focusing only on functions and logic, OOP focuses on modeling real-world entities.

Examples:

• Student
• Car
• Bank Account
• User
• AI Model
• Robot

Each object contains:

• Data (attributes)
• Actions (methods)

-----------------------------------

**Real-World Analogy of OOP**

Think about a car.

A car has:

Attributes:

• Color
• Brand
• Speed
• Fuel level

Actions:

• Start()
• Stop()
• Accelerate()
• Brake()

In OOP:

We model this using:

• Class → blueprint
• Object → actual car

-----------------------------------

**Why OOP is Important**

OOP provides many advantages.

-----------------------------------

**1. Code Reusability**

Code can be reused multiple times.

-----------------------------------

**2. Better Organization**

Large applications become modular.

-----------------------------------

**3. Easier Maintenance**

Updating one class updates all related objects.

-----------------------------------

**4. Real-World Modeling**

Programs become easier to design.

-----------------------------------

**5. Scalability**

OOP supports large enterprise applications.

-----------------------------------

**6. Important in AI & ML**

AI frameworks use OOP extensively.

Examples:

• Neural network classes
• Dataset classes
• Model classes
• Prediction objects

-----------------------------------

# CLASS AND OBJECTS

-----------------------------------

**What is a Class?**

A class is a blueprint or template used to create objects.

It defines:

• Attributes
• Methods

Example:

A class Student defines:

• Name
• Age
• Marks

-----------------------------------

**What is an Object?**

An object is a real instance created from a class.

Example:

If Student is a class:

Rahul and Priya are objects.

-----------------------------------

**Understanding Blueprint Analogy**

Think of a class like a building blueprint.

The blueprint defines:

• Structure
• Design
• Features

But the actual building is the object.

-----------------------------------

**Creating a Class in Python**

Python uses the class keyword.

Example:

class Student:
    pass

Explanation:

• class → keyword
• Student → class name
• pass → placeholder

-----------------------------------

**Naming Convention for Classes**

Python follows:

PascalCase.

Example:

BankAccount

StudentManager

AIModel

-----------------------------------

# CREATING OBJECTS

-----------------------------------

Objects are created using the class name.

Example:

class Student:
    pass

s1 = Student()

Explanation:

• s1 is an object
• Student() creates the object

-----------------------------------

**Printing an Object**

Example:

print(s1)

Python displays:

Memory location of the object.

-----------------------------------

# ATTRIBUTES

-----------------------------------

**What are Attributes?**

Attributes are variables inside objects.

They store object data.

Example:

• Student name
• Student marks
• Account balance

-----------------------------------

**Creating Attributes**

Example:

class Student:
    pass

s1 = Student()

s1.name = "Rahul"
s1.age = 20

print(s1.name)

Output:

Rahul

-----------------------------------

**Why Attributes are Important**

Attributes store object-specific information.

Each object can have different values.

Example:

Student 1:

• Rahul
• Age 20

Student 2:

• Priya
• Age 21

-----------------------------------

# METHODS

-----------------------------------

**What are Methods?**

Methods are functions inside classes.

Methods define object behavior.

Example:

A BankAccount object may have:

• deposit()
• withdraw()
• check_balance()

-----------------------------------

**Creating Methods**

Example:

class Student:

    def greet(self):
        print("Hello Student")

s1 = Student()

s1.greet()

Output:

Hello Student

-----------------------------------

# THE self KEYWORD

-----------------------------------

**What is self?**

self refers to the current object.

It allows objects to access:

• Attributes
• Methods

Every instance method must contain self as the first parameter.

-----------------------------------

**Why self is Important**

Without self:

Python cannot identify which object's data should be used.

-----------------------------------

**Example of self**

class Student:

    def set_name(self, name):
        self.name = name

s1 = Student()

s1.set_name("Rahul")

print(s1.name)

Output:

Rahul

-----------------------------------

**Understanding self Step-by-Step**

When:

s1.set_name("Rahul")

is called:

Python automatically sends:

self = s1

So:

self.name

means:

s1.name

-----------------------------------

# CONSTRUCTOR (__init__)

-----------------------------------

**What is a Constructor?**

A constructor is a special method automatically executed when an object is created.

Python uses:

__init__()

as constructor.

-----------------------------------

**Why Constructors are Important**

Constructors initialize object data automatically.

Without constructors:

Attributes must be assigned manually.

-----------------------------------

**Creating Constructor**

Example:

class Student:

    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

s1 = Student("Rahul", 20)

print(s1.name)

Output:

Rahul

-----------------------------------

**Understanding Constructor Flow**

When object is created:

s1 = Student("Rahul", 20)

Python automatically calls:

__init__()

-----------------------------------

**Constructor Parameters**

Constructors can receive:

• Name
• Age
• Balance
• Email
• Any required data

-----------------------------------

**Multiple Objects Example**

class Student:

    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

s1 = Student("Rahul", 20)
s2 = Student("Priya", 21)

Each object stores different data.

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: BANK ACCOUNT CLASS

-----------------------------------

Example:

class BankAccount:

    def __init__(self, name, balance):
        self.name = name
        self.balance = balance

    def deposit(self, amount):
        self.balance += amount

    def withdraw(self, amount):
        self.balance -= amount

    def display(self):
        print(self.name)
        print(self.balance)

account1 = BankAccount("Rahul", 5000)

account1.deposit(1000)
account1.withdraw(500)

account1.display()

-----------------------------------

**Output**

Rahul
5500

-----------------------------------

# LIBRARY MANAGEMENT CLASS EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Example:

class Library:

    def __init__(self):
        self.books = []

    def add_book(self, book):
        self.books.append(book)

    def show_books(self):
        print(self.books)

-----------------------------------

# INSTANCE VARIABLES

-----------------------------------

Variables attached to objects are called:

Instance Variables.

Example:

self.name
self.age
self.balance

Each object gets separate copies.

-----------------------------------

# OBJECT MEMORY CONCEPT

-----------------------------------

Every object occupies memory separately.

Example:

s1 and s2 are different objects.

Changing s1 does not affect s2.

-----------------------------------

# OOP IN AI AND MACHINE LEARNING

-----------------------------------

AI frameworks heavily use OOP.

Examples:

TensorFlow:

• Model classes
• Layer classes

PyTorch:

• Neural network classes
• Tensor objects

Scikit-learn:

• Classifier objects
• Regression objects

-----------------------------------

**Why AI Developers Need OOP**

OOP helps AI developers:

• Organize models
• Reuse training logic
• Build scalable systems
• Manage datasets
• Build production-ready applications

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD APPLICATIONS OF OOP

-----------------------------------

OOP is used in:

• Banking systems
• Mobile applications
• Web applications
• AI systems
• Robotics
• Gaming
• Operating systems
• Automation software

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Forgetting self parameter

Every instance method needs self.

-----------------------------------

2. Forgetting to create objects

Class alone does nothing.

-----------------------------------

3. Confusing class and object

Class = blueprint

Object = real instance

-----------------------------------

4. Incorrect indentation

Python requires proper indentation.

-----------------------------------

5. Accessing undefined attributes

Attributes must exist before use.

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Use meaningful class names

Good Example:

BankAccount

Bad Example:

Class1

-----------------------------------

2. Keep methods focused

Each method should perform one task.

-----------------------------------

3. Use constructors properly

Initialize required data inside __init__.

-----------------------------------

4. Avoid unnecessary global variables

-----------------------------------

5. Follow clean code practices

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What Object-Oriented Programming is
• Importance of OOP
• Classes and objects
• Attributes and methods
• The self keyword
• Constructors using __init__
• Instance variables
• Real-world OOP examples
• OOP usage in AI and Machine Learning

Object-Oriented Programming is one of the most important concepts in modern software development and Artificial Intelligence. Mastering OOP helps developers build scalable, reusable, and professional applications.
keyPoints: OOP organizes programs using classes and objects
A class is a blueprint for creating objects
Objects are instances of classes
Attributes store object data
Methods define object behavior
self refers to the current object
__init__ is the constructor in Python
Constructors initialize object attributes automatically
Instance variables belong to specific objects
OOP improves code reusability and maintainability
AI frameworks heavily use Object-Oriented Programming
Proper OOP design helps build scalable applications
