-----------------------------------

# RANDOM MODULE IN NUMPY

-----------------------------------

Random numbers are extremely important in AI.

Used for:

• Dataset generation
• Neural network initialization
• Simulations
• Random sampling

-----------------------------------

# RANDOM INTEGER GENERATION

-----------------------------------

Example:

arr = np.random.randint(1, 10, size=5)

print(arr)

-----------------------------------

# RANDOM FLOAT VALUES

-----------------------------------

Example:

arr = np.random.rand(3)

-----------------------------------

# RANDOM 2D ARRAY

-----------------------------------

Example:

arr = np.random.rand(2, 3)

-----------------------------------

# RANDOM NORMAL DISTRIBUTION

-----------------------------------

Used heavily in Machine Learning.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

arr = np.random.randn(5)

-----------------------------------

# RANDOM SEED

-----------------------------------

Random seed ensures reproducible results.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

np.random.seed(42)

-----------------------------------

# WHY RANDOM SEED IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Without seed:

Different outputs every run.

With seed:

Same random output every time.

Useful for:

• AI experiments
• Reproducibility
• Research

-----------------------------------

# DATA DISTRIBUTION

-----------------------------------

Statistics helps understand:

• How data behaves
• Whether data is balanced
• Presence of outliers

-----------------------------------

# OUTLIERS

-----------------------------------

Outliers are abnormal values.

Example:

Most marks:

70-90

One value:

1000

That value is an outlier.

-----------------------------------

# WHY OUTLIERS MATTER

-----------------------------------

Outliers can:

• Distort AI models
• Reduce accuracy
• Affect predictions

-----------------------------------

# NUMPY IN MACHINE LEARNING

-----------------------------------

Machine Learning models depend on:

• Statistics
• Matrix operations
• Random initialization

Examples:

• Neural networks
• Linear regression
• Recommendation systems
• AI predictions

-----------------------------------

# AI EXAMPLE USING STATISTICS

-----------------------------------

Student dataset:

• Marks
• Attendance
• Study hours

Statistics helps:

• Find average performance
• Identify weak students
• Detect unusual patterns

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Confusing mean and median

-----------------------------------

2. Forgetting axis parameter

-----------------------------------

3. Incorrect matrix dimensions

-----------------------------------

4. Using random values without seed

-----------------------------------

5. Ignoring outliers in datasets

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Analyze data statistically before ML training

-----------------------------------

2. Use random seeds for reproducibility

-----------------------------------

3. Understand matrix dimensions clearly

-----------------------------------

4. Normalize data before training AI models

-----------------------------------

5. Detect and handle outliers properly

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• Aggregation functions
• sum(), min(), max()
• Mean and median
• Standard deviation and variance
• Axis parameter
• Matrix operations
• Matrix multiplication
• Transpose and inverse
• NumPy random module
• Random seed
• Importance of statistics in AI

Statistics and linear algebra are the foundation of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. Almost every AI algorithm depends on matrix operations, statistical analysis, and random number generation for training and prediction.
keyPoints: Aggregation functions summarize numerical data
Mean calculates the average value
Median represents the middle value
Standard deviation measures data spread
Variance measures distribution variability
Axis parameter controls row-wise or column-wise operations
Matrix operations are essential in Machine Learning
Matrix multiplication is heavily used in neural networks
NumPy random module generates random datasets
Random seed ensures reproducible outputs
Statistics helps analyze and preprocess AI datasets
Linear algebra forms the mathematical foundation of AI
content.practicalTask: Create a student marks dataset using NumPy arrays and calculate mean, median, standard deviation, variance, minimum, maximum, and row-wise/column-wise statistics.
content.assignment: Generate a random dataset using NumPy random functions and perform a complete statistical analysis including mean, median, standard deviation, variance, and matrix operations.
content.miniProject: Build a Student Data Analytics System using NumPy that generates random student records, calculates full statistical summaries, identifies toppers and weak students, and performs matrix-based analysis on the dataset.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Matrix Operations with NumPy**

Matrices are one of the most important mathematical concepts in Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Computer Vision, Robotics, and Data Science.

Almost every AI algorithm internally works using:

• Matrices
• Vectors
• Linear algebra operations

When AI models process:

• Images
• Videos
• Neural network weights
• Datasets
• Predictions

They are actually performing matrix calculations.

This is why understanding matrix operations is essential for every AI Engineer and Data Scientist.

NumPy provides powerful tools for working with matrices efficiently.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A MATRIX?

-----------------------------------

A matrix is a rectangular arrangement of numbers in rows and columns.

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Example:

A = [
    [1, 2, 3],
    [4, 5, 6]
]

This matrix contains:

• 2 rows
• 3 columns

-----------------------------------

# WHY MATRICES ARE IMPORTANT IN AI

-----------------------------------

Matrices are used in:

• Neural networks
• Image processing
• Recommendation systems
• Deep learning
• Computer graphics
• Robotics
• Scientific simulations

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD AI EXAMPLES

-----------------------------------

# IMAGE PROCESSING

-----------------------------------

An image is represented as a matrix of pixels.

Grayscale image:

2D matrix.

Color image:

3D matrix.

-----------------------------------

# NEURAL NETWORKS

-----------------------------------

Neural networks use:

• Matrix multiplication
• Weight matrices
• Input matrices

for predictions and learning.

-----------------------------------

# CREATING MATRICES IN NUMPY

-----------------------------------

Matrices are created using:

np.array()

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX CREATION EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

import numpy as np

A = np.array([
    [1, 2],
    [3, 4]
])

print(A)

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX DIMENSIONS

-----------------------------------

Matrix dimensions are:

Rows × Columns

-----------------------------------

# CHECKING MATRIX SHAPE

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(A.shape)

Output:

(2, 2)

-----------------------------------

# SPECIAL MATRICES

-----------------------------------

NumPy provides functions for creating special matrices.

-----------------------------------

# ZERO MATRIX

-----------------------------------

Contains only zeros.

-----------------------------------

**Example**

matrix = np.zeros((3, 3))

-----------------------------------

# ONES MATRIX

-----------------------------------

Contains only ones.

-----------------------------------

**Example**

matrix = np.ones((2, 2))

-----------------------------------

# IDENTITY MATRIX

-----------------------------------

An identity matrix contains:

• 1 on diagonal
• 0 elsewhere

-----------------------------------

# IDENTITY MATRIX EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

I = np.eye(3)

print(I)

-----------------------------------

# WHY IDENTITY MATRIX IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used in:

• Linear algebra
• Neural networks
• Matrix inversion
• AI computations

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX ADDITION

-----------------------------------

Matrices of same dimensions can be added.

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX ADDITION FORMULA

-----------------------------------



-----------------------------------

# MATRIX ADDITION EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

A = np.array([
    [1, 2],
    [3, 4]
])

B = np.array([
    [5, 6],
    [7, 8]
])

print(A + B)

Output:

[[ 6  8]
 [10 12]]

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX SUBTRACTION

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(B - A)

-----------------------------------

# SCALAR MULTIPLICATION

-----------------------------------

Each element is multiplied by a number.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(A * 2)

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX MULTIPLICATION

-----------------------------------

Matrix multiplication is one of the most important operations in AI.

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX MULTIPLICATION RULE

-----------------------------------

Two matrices can multiply if:

Columns of first matrix = Rows of second matrix.

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX MULTIPLICATION FORMULA

-----------------------------------



-----------------------------------

# MATRIX MULTIPLICATION EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

A = np.array([
    [1, 2],
    [3, 4]
])

B = np.array([
    [5, 6],
    [7, 8]
])

print(np.dot(A, B))

-----------------------------------

# np.dot()

-----------------------------------

Performs matrix multiplication.

-----------------------------------

# np.matmul()

-----------------------------------

Another function for matrix multiplication.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(np.matmul(A, B))

-----------------------------------

# WHY MATRIX MULTIPLICATION MATTERS

-----------------------------------

Neural networks perform millions of matrix multiplications.

Used in:

• AI predictions
• Deep learning
• Image recognition
• NLP models

-----------------------------------

# MANUAL MATRIX MULTIPLICATION

-----------------------------------

Example:

For:

A = [[1,2],[3,4]]

B = [[5,6],[7,8]]

Result:

[[19,22],[43,50]]

Calculation:

(1×5 + 2×7) = 19

(1×6 + 2×8) = 22

-----------------------------------

# TRANSPOSE OF MATRIX

-----------------------------------

Transpose converts:

Rows → Columns

Columns → Rows

-----------------------------------

# TRANSPOSE FORMULA

-----------------------------------



-----------------------------------

# TRANSPOSE EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

A = np.array([
    [1, 2, 3],
    [4, 5, 6]
])

print(A.T)

Output:

[[1 4]
 [2 5]
 [3 6]]

-----------------------------------

# WHY TRANSPOSE IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used in:

• Neural network calculations
• Data transformations
• Matrix algebra
• AI optimization

-----------------------------------

# DETERMINANT OF MATRIX

-----------------------------------

Determinant is a special value calculated from a matrix.

-----------------------------------

# DETERMINANT FORMULA

-----------------------------------

For 2×2 matrix:



-----------------------------------

# DETERMINANT EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

A = np.array([
    [1, 2],
    [3, 4]
])

print(np.linalg.det(A))

-----------------------------------

# WHY DETERMINANT IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Determines:

• Matrix invertibility
• Linear independence
• Stability of equations

-----------------------------------

# INVERSE OF MATRIX

-----------------------------------

Inverse matrix reverses matrix operations.

-----------------------------------

# INVERSE FORMULA

-----------------------------------



-----------------------------------

# INVERSE MATRIX EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

A = np.array([
    [1, 2],
    [3, 4]
])

print(np.linalg.inv(A))

-----------------------------------

# WHY INVERSE MATRICES MATTER

-----------------------------------

Used in:

• Solving equations
• Machine Learning
• Linear regression
• AI optimization

-----------------------------------

# SOLVING LINEAR EQUATIONS

-----------------------------------

AI systems often solve equations.

-----------------------------------

# LINEAR EQUATION EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Equations:

2x + y = 5

x + 3y = 6

-----------------------------------

# MATRIX REPRESENTATION

-----------------------------------



-----------------------------------

# SOLVING USING NUMPY

-----------------------------------

A = np.array([
    [2, 1],
    [1, 3]
])

B = np.array([5, 6])

X = np.linalg.solve(A, B)

print(X)

-----------------------------------

# WHY LINEAR ALGEBRA IS IMPORTANT IN AI

-----------------------------------

Machine Learning algorithms rely heavily on:

• Matrix multiplication
• Matrix inversion
• Vector operations
• Linear transformations

-----------------------------------

# MATRICES IN DEEP LEARNING

-----------------------------------

Neural networks use matrices for:

• Inputs
• Weights
• Activations
• Predictions

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE OF AI DATA AS MATRIX

-----------------------------------

Student dataset:

| Math | Science | English |
|------|----------|----------|
| 90 | 80 | 70 |
| 85 | 95 | 88 |

Can be stored as matrix.

-----------------------------------

# VECTOR VS MATRIX

-----------------------------------

Vector:

Single row or column.

Matrix:

Multiple rows and columns.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Confusing element-wise multiplication with matrix multiplication

-----------------------------------

2. Incorrect matrix dimensions

-----------------------------------

3. Forgetting transpose requirements

-----------------------------------

4. Trying to invert singular matrices

-----------------------------------

5. Mixing lists and NumPy arrays

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Always check matrix dimensions before operations

-----------------------------------

2. Use np.dot() or np.matmul() for matrix multiplication

-----------------------------------

3. Understand difference between scalar and matrix operations

-----------------------------------

4. Use NumPy instead of manual loops for efficiency

-----------------------------------

5. Practice solving equations using matrices

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD AI APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

Matrix operations are used in:

• Face recognition
• ChatGPT-like models
• Image classification
• Recommendation systems
• Speech recognition
• Robotics

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What matrices are
• Matrix creation in NumPy
• Matrix dimensions
• Matrix addition and subtraction
• Scalar multiplication
• Matrix multiplication
• np.dot() and np.matmul()
• Matrix transpose
• Determinant calculation
• Matrix inverse
• Solving linear equations
• Importance of matrices in AI and Machine Learning

Matrix operations and linear algebra form the mathematical backbone of Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning. Almost every modern AI system depends on efficient matrix computations for training and prediction.
keyPoints: Matrices are rectangular arrangements of numbers
Matrix operations are fundamental to AI and Machine Learning
NumPy provides efficient matrix computation functions
np.dot() and np.matmul() perform matrix multiplication
Transpose converts rows into columns
Determinant determines matrix properties
Inverse matrices help solve linear equations
Identity matrices are important in linear algebra
Neural networks rely heavily on matrix multiplication
Linear algebra is the mathematical foundation of Deep Learning
Matrix dimensions must be compatible for multiplication
NumPy matrix operations are optimized and efficient
content.practicalTask: Implement matrix multiplication manually using loops and compare the output with NumPy's np.dot() and np.matmul() functions.
content.assignment: Solve a system of linear equations using NumPy matrix operations and explain how matrices are used in Machine Learning.
content.miniProject: Build a Simple AI Math Toolkit using NumPy that performs matrix addition, subtraction, multiplication, transpose, determinant calculation, inverse calculation, and solves systems of linear equations.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Working with Real Datasets Using NumPy**

In real-world Artificial Intelligence and Data Science projects, data rarely comes in a clean and ready-to-use format.

Data scientists spend a huge amount of time:

• Loading datasets
• Cleaning data
• Transforming values
• Reshaping arrays
• Combining datasets
• Splitting datasets
• Preparing data for Machine Learning models

This process is called:

Data Preprocessing.

Data preprocessing is one of the most important stages in AI and Machine Learning.

Even the best Machine Learning model performs poorly if the dataset is:

• Dirty
• Incomplete
• Incorrectly formatted
• Unbalanced
• Improperly structured

NumPy provides powerful tools for handling real-world numerical datasets efficiently.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A DATASET?

-----------------------------------

A dataset is a collection of data.

Examples:

• Student marks
• Medical reports
• Sales records
• Sensor readings
• Stock market data
• AI training images
• Customer analytics

-----------------------------------

# DATASETS IN AI

-----------------------------------

AI systems learn patterns from datasets.

Examples:

• Face recognition systems train on image datasets
• Chatbots train on text datasets
• Recommendation systems train on user behavior datasets

-----------------------------------

# WHY DATA PREPROCESSING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Raw data often contains:

• Missing values
• Duplicate values
• Incorrect formats
• Noise
• Outliers

Machine Learning models require:

• Clean data
• Structured data
• Numerical representation

-----------------------------------

# LOADING DATA WITH NUMPY

-----------------------------------

NumPy provides functions for loading datasets.

-----------------------------------

# np.loadtxt()

-----------------------------------

Used for loading numerical data from text files.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

import numpy as np

data = np.loadtxt('data.txt')

print(data)

-----------------------------------

# CSV FILES

-----------------------------------

CSV means:

Comma Separated Values.

Example:

Name,Math,Science

John,90,85

Alice,88,92

-----------------------------------

# LOADING CSV DATA

-----------------------------------

Example:

data = np.loadtxt('students.csv', delimiter=',')

-----------------------------------

# SKIPPING HEADER ROWS

-----------------------------------

Many datasets contain headers.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

data = np.loadtxt(
    'students.csv',
    delimiter=',',
    skiprows=1
)

-----------------------------------

# np.genfromtxt()

-----------------------------------

More flexible than loadtxt().

Useful for datasets containing:

• Missing values
• Mixed data
• Invalid entries

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

data = np.genfromtxt(
    'students.csv',
    delimiter=',',
    skip_header=1
)

-----------------------------------

# VIEWING DATASET SHAPE

-----------------------------------

Understanding dataset dimensions is important.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(data.shape)

Output:

(rows, columns)

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS RESHAPING?

-----------------------------------

Reshaping changes array dimensions without changing data.

-----------------------------------

# WHY RESHAPING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Machine Learning models often require data in specific shapes.

Examples:

• Neural networks
• Image processing
• Deep learning models

-----------------------------------

# reshape() FUNCTION

-----------------------------------

Changes array dimensions.

-----------------------------------

# RESHAPE EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

arr = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6])

reshaped = arr.reshape(2, 3)

print(reshaped)

Output:

[[1 2 3]
 [4 5 6]]

-----------------------------------

# RESHAPING RULE

-----------------------------------

Total elements must remain same.

Example:

6 elements:

Possible shapes:

• 2×3
• 3×2
• 1×6
• 6×1

-----------------------------------

# FLATTENING ARRAYS

-----------------------------------

Flattening converts multi-dimensional arrays into 1D arrays.

-----------------------------------

# flatten() FUNCTION

-----------------------------------

Example:

matrix = np.array([
    [1, 2],
    [3, 4]
])

print(matrix.flatten())

Output:

[1 2 3 4]

-----------------------------------

# WHY FLATTENING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used in:

• Neural networks
• Feature extraction
• AI preprocessing
• Data transformation

-----------------------------------

# STACKING ARRAYS

-----------------------------------

Stacking combines arrays together.

-----------------------------------

# HORIZONTAL STACKING

-----------------------------------

Combines arrays column-wise.

-----------------------------------

# hstack() EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

A = np.array([1, 2, 3])
B = np.array([4, 5, 6])

print(np.hstack((A, B)))

Output:

[1 2 3 4 5 6]

-----------------------------------

# VERTICAL STACKING

-----------------------------------

Combines arrays row-wise.

-----------------------------------

# vstack() EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(np.vstack((A, B)))

Output:

[[1 2 3]
 [4 5 6]]

-----------------------------------

# WHY STACKING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used for:

• Combining datasets
• Merging features
• Building AI training data
• Data engineering

-----------------------------------

# SPLITTING ARRAYS

-----------------------------------

Splitting divides arrays into smaller parts.

-----------------------------------

# split() FUNCTION

-----------------------------------

Example:

arr = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6])

print(np.split(arr, 3))

-----------------------------------

# WHY SPLITTING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used in:

• Training/testing datasets
• Batch processing
• AI model evaluation

-----------------------------------

# DATA CLEANING

-----------------------------------

Data cleaning improves dataset quality.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON DATA CLEANING TASKS

-----------------------------------

• Removing missing values
• Handling duplicates
• Filtering invalid data
• Normalization
• Feature scaling

-----------------------------------

# REMOVING INVALID VALUES

-----------------------------------

Example:

arr = np.array([10, -1, 20, -5, 30])

cleaned = arr[arr >= 0]

print(cleaned)

-----------------------------------

# HANDLING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Missing values can break ML models.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

arr = np.array([1, np.nan, 3])

print(np.nan_to_num(arr))

-----------------------------------

# NORMALIZATION

-----------------------------------

Normalization scales values into standard ranges.

-----------------------------------

# NORMALIZATION FORMULA

-----------------------------------



-----------------------------------

# NORMALIZATION EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

arr = np.array([10, 20, 30, 40])

normalized = (arr - arr.min()) / (arr.max() - arr.min())

print(normalized)

-----------------------------------

# WHY NORMALIZATION MATTERS

-----------------------------------

Machine Learning models perform better when:

• Features have similar ranges
• Data is scaled properly

-----------------------------------

# ROW-WISE OPERATIONS

-----------------------------------

NumPy can calculate row-wise statistics.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

matrix = np.array([
    [80, 90, 85],
    [70, 75, 65]
])

print(np.mean(matrix, axis=1))

-----------------------------------

# COLUMN-WISE OPERATIONS

-----------------------------------

Column-wise operations analyze features.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(np.mean(matrix, axis=0))

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD AI DATASET EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Student Dataset:

| Math | Science | English |
|------|----------|----------|
| 90 | 85 | 88 |
| 70 | 60 | 75 |

AI systems analyze:

• Average marks
• Performance trends
• Weak subjects
• Student rankings

-----------------------------------

# IMAGE DATASETS IN AI

-----------------------------------

Images are stored as NumPy arrays.

Example:

A 28×28 grayscale image:

28×28 matrix.

-----------------------------------

# TRAINING & TESTING DATA

-----------------------------------

Datasets are usually split into:

• Training data
• Testing data

-----------------------------------

# WHY SPLITTING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Training data:

Used for learning.

Testing data:

Used for evaluation.

-----------------------------------

# AI DATA PIPELINE

-----------------------------------

Real AI systems follow steps:

Dataset → Cleaning → Transformation → Normalization → Model Training

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Incorrect reshape dimensions

-----------------------------------

2. Ignoring missing values

-----------------------------------

3. Mixing incompatible array shapes

-----------------------------------

4. Forgetting normalization

-----------------------------------

5. Using loops instead of NumPy operations

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Always inspect dataset shape

-----------------------------------

2. Clean data before training models

-----------------------------------

3. Normalize numerical features

-----------------------------------

4. Use vectorized NumPy operations

-----------------------------------

5. Split training and testing data properly

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

NumPy preprocessing is used in:

• AI chatbots
• Face recognition
• Medical AI systems
• Fraud detection
• Recommendation systems
• Autonomous vehicles

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• Loading datasets using NumPy
• np.loadtxt() and np.genfromtxt()
• Dataset preprocessing
• Reshaping arrays
• Flattening arrays
• Stacking arrays
• Splitting arrays
• Data cleaning
• Handling missing values
• Normalization
• Row-wise and column-wise statistics
• Real-world AI dataset processing

Data preprocessing is one of the most critical stages in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. Clean and properly structured data helps AI models achieve better accuracy, performance, and reliability.
keyPoints: Datasets are collections of structured data used for AI training
NumPy provides tools for loading and preprocessing datasets
np.loadtxt() loads numerical datasets from files
reshape() changes array dimensions without changing data
flatten() converts multi-dimensional arrays into 1D arrays
hstack() and vstack() combine arrays
split() divides arrays into smaller sections
Data cleaning removes invalid or missing values
Normalization scales data into standard ranges
Row-wise and column-wise operations help analyze datasets
Proper preprocessing improves Machine Learning performance
Real-world AI systems rely heavily on data preprocessing
content.practicalTask: Load a CSV dataset using NumPy, reshape arrays, clean invalid data, and calculate row-wise and column-wise statistics.
content.assignment: Preprocess a raw numerical dataset by handling missing values, normalizing features, reshaping arrays, and preparing it for Machine Learning readiness.
content.miniProject: Build a Statistical Data Analyzer using NumPy that loads datasets, cleans data, calculates statistical summaries, reshapes arrays, performs normalization, and generates insights from real-world numerical datasets.

weeks.title: Pandas & Data Visualization
weeks.assignment: Perform full exploratory data analysis (EDA) on a real-world CSV dataset.
weeks.miniProject: Exploratory Data Analysis Dashboard on a Public Dataset (e.g., Titanic or Iris).

content.theory:
**Introduction to Pandas for Data Analysis**

In Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, and Data Science, working with data efficiently is one of the most important skills.

Real-world datasets are often:

• Large
• Messy
• Unorganized
• Complex

Handling such data using normal Python lists becomes difficult.

This is where Pandas becomes extremely powerful.

Pandas is one of the most important Python libraries used in:

• Data Analysis
• Machine Learning
• Artificial Intelligence
• Data Cleaning
• Data Visualization
• Business Analytics

Pandas provides powerful data structures and tools for handling structured data quickly and efficiently.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS PANDAS?

-----------------------------------

Pandas is an open-source Python library used for:

• Reading datasets
• Cleaning data
• Organizing data
• Filtering data
• Analyzing data
• Manipulating tables

Pandas is built on top of NumPy.

This means:

• NumPy handles numerical computations
• Pandas handles structured data analysis

-----------------------------------

# WHY PANDAS IS IMPORTANT IN AI

-----------------------------------

Before training Machine Learning models, data scientists use Pandas to:

• Clean datasets
• Remove missing values
• Analyze patterns
• Prepare features
• Filter useful data
• Transform raw data into ML-ready format

Without Pandas, data preprocessing becomes difficult and time-consuming.

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD USE CASES OF PANDAS

-----------------------------------

Pandas is used in:

• Netflix recommendation systems
• Financial analytics
• Healthcare AI systems
• Fraud detection
• Stock market prediction
• Customer behavior analysis
• AI chatbot training datasets

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING PANDAS

-----------------------------------

Pandas can be installed using pip.

-----------------------------------

# INSTALL COMMAND

-----------------------------------

pip install pandas

-----------------------------------

# IMPORTING PANDAS

-----------------------------------

Pandas is commonly imported as:

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

import pandas as pd

-----------------------------------

# TWO MAIN DATA STRUCTURES IN PANDAS

-----------------------------------

Pandas mainly provides:

1. Series

2. DataFrame

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A SERIES?

-----------------------------------

A Series is a one-dimensional labeled array.

It is similar to:

• Python list
• NumPy 1D array

but with labels (indexes).

-----------------------------------

# SERIES EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

import pandas as pd

numbers = pd.Series([10, 20, 30, 40])

print(numbers)

-----------------------------------

# OUTPUT

-----------------------------------

0    10
1    20
2    30
3    40

dtype: int64

-----------------------------------

# SERIES WITH CUSTOM INDEX

-----------------------------------

Example:

marks = pd.Series(
    [90, 85, 88],
    index=['Math', 'Science', 'English']
)

print(marks)

-----------------------------------

# ACCESSING SERIES VALUES

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(marks['Math'])

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A DATAFRAME?

-----------------------------------

A DataFrame is a two-dimensional table.

It contains:

• Rows
• Columns

similar to:

• Excel sheets
• SQL tables
• CSV files

-----------------------------------

# DATAFRAME EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

data = {
    'Name': ['John', 'Alice', 'Bob'],
    'Marks': [90, 85, 88],
    'City': ['Delhi', 'Mumbai', 'Bangalore']
}

students = pd.DataFrame(data)

print(students)

-----------------------------------

# WHY DATAFRAMES ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Almost every Machine Learning dataset is handled as a DataFrame.

Examples:

• Student records
• Medical datasets
• Customer analytics
• Sales reports
• AI training data

-----------------------------------

# VIEWING DATAFRAME INFORMATION

-----------------------------------

Pandas provides many functions for exploring datasets.

-----------------------------------

# head()

-----------------------------------

Shows first 5 rows.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(students.head())

-----------------------------------

# tail()

-----------------------------------

Shows last 5 rows.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(students.tail())

-----------------------------------

# shape

-----------------------------------

Shows dataset dimensions.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(students.shape)

Output:

(rows, columns)

-----------------------------------

# columns

-----------------------------------

Displays column names.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(students.columns)

-----------------------------------

# dtypes

-----------------------------------

Shows data types of each column.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(students.dtypes)

-----------------------------------

# info()

-----------------------------------

Provides complete dataset summary.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(students.info())

-----------------------------------

# WHAT info() SHOWS

-----------------------------------

• Number of rows
• Number of columns
• Data types
• Missing values
• Memory usage

-----------------------------------

# describe()

-----------------------------------

Generates statistical summary.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(students.describe())

-----------------------------------

# WHAT describe() SHOWS

-----------------------------------

• Mean
• Median
• Standard deviation
• Minimum value
• Maximum value
• Percentiles

-----------------------------------

# READING CSV FILES

-----------------------------------

Most AI datasets are stored as CSV files.

-----------------------------------

# read_csv()

-----------------------------------

Used to load CSV datasets.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

df = pd.read_csv('students.csv')

print(df.head())

-----------------------------------

# WHY CSV FILES ARE COMMON

-----------------------------------

CSV files are:

• Lightweight
• Easy to share
• Supported by almost every platform

-----------------------------------

# READING EXCEL FILES

-----------------------------------

Pandas can also read Excel files.

-----------------------------------

# read_excel()

-----------------------------------

Example:

df = pd.read_excel('students.xlsx')

-----------------------------------

# SELECTING COLUMNS

-----------------------------------

Columns can be selected easily.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df['Name'])

-----------------------------------

# SELECTING MULTIPLE COLUMNS

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(df[['Name', 'Marks']])

-----------------------------------

# SELECTING ROWS

-----------------------------------

Rows are selected using:

• loc[]
• iloc[]

-----------------------------------

# loc[]

-----------------------------------

Uses labels.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df.loc[0])

-----------------------------------

# iloc[]

-----------------------------------

Uses index positions.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df.iloc[0])

-----------------------------------

# FILTERING DATA

-----------------------------------

Filtering is one of the most useful Pandas features.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

high_marks = df[df['Marks'] > 85]

print(high_marks)

-----------------------------------

# WHY FILTERING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used in:

• Customer segmentation
• Fraud detection
• Medical analysis
• AI predictions

-----------------------------------

# ADDING NEW COLUMNS

-----------------------------------

Example:

df['Pass'] = df['Marks'] > 40

-----------------------------------

# MODIFYING DATA

-----------------------------------

Example:

df['Marks'] = df['Marks'] + 5

-----------------------------------

# HANDLING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Datasets often contain missing values.

-----------------------------------

# CHECKING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(df.isnull())

-----------------------------------

# COUNTING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(df.isnull().sum())

-----------------------------------

# REMOVING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Example:

cleaned = df.dropna()

-----------------------------------

# FILLING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Example:

filled = df.fillna(0)

-----------------------------------

# SORTING DATA

-----------------------------------

Example:

sorted_df = df.sort_values(by='Marks')

-----------------------------------

# GROUPING DATA

-----------------------------------

Grouping helps analyze categories.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df.groupby('City')['Marks'].mean())

-----------------------------------

# WHY GROUPING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used in:

• Sales analysis
• AI analytics
• Business intelligence
• Trend analysis

-----------------------------------

# EXPORTING DATA

-----------------------------------

Pandas can save processed datasets.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

df.to_csv('output.csv', index=False)

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD AI WORKFLOW

-----------------------------------

AI projects often follow:

Dataset → Pandas Cleaning → Feature Engineering → ML Model Training

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Forgetting parentheses in functions

-----------------------------------

2. Using wrong column names

-----------------------------------

3. Ignoring missing values

-----------------------------------

4. Confusing loc[] and iloc[]

-----------------------------------

5. Accidentally modifying original datasets

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Always inspect dataset using head() and info()

-----------------------------------

2. Handle missing values carefully

-----------------------------------

3. Use meaningful column names

-----------------------------------

4. Keep original dataset backup

-----------------------------------

5. Use Pandas instead of loops for efficiency

-----------------------------------

# PANDAS IN MACHINE LEARNING

-----------------------------------

Pandas is used before training ML models for:

• Data cleaning
• Feature selection
• Data transformation
• Dataset analysis
• Data visualization preparation

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What Pandas is
• Why Pandas is important in AI
• Series and DataFrames
• Creating DataFrames
• Reading CSV and Excel files
• Exploring datasets using head(), tail(), info(), describe()
• Selecting rows and columns
• Filtering datasets
• Handling missing values
• Sorting and grouping data
• Exporting datasets
• Real-world AI preprocessing workflows

Pandas is one of the most essential libraries for Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, and Data Science. Mastering Pandas helps you efficiently handle real-world datasets and prepares you for advanced AI model development.
keyPoints: Pandas is a powerful library for data analysis and preprocessing
Series is a one-dimensional labeled array
DataFrame is a two-dimensional tabular structure
read_csv() loads CSV datasets efficiently
head(), tail(), info(), and describe() help explore datasets
Filtering and grouping help analyze structured data
Missing values must be handled before ML training
Pandas works closely with NumPy for AI workflows
Data preprocessing is critical for Machine Learning success
DataFrames are widely used in real-world AI systems
Sorting, grouping, and transformation improve analysis quality
Pandas is essential for data science and AI development
content.practicalTask: Load a CSV dataset using Pandas and explore its structure using head(), tail(), info(), shape, columns, and describe().
content.assignment: Load a real-world dataset and generate a complete summary including missing values, data types, statistical analysis, and grouped insights.
content.miniProject: Build a Student Performance Analyzer using Pandas that loads CSV data, filters records, calculates averages, groups students by category, handles missing values, and exports processed reports.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Data Cleaning & Preprocessing**

In Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning, raw data is rarely perfect.

Real-world datasets often contain:

• Missing values
• Incorrect data
• Duplicate records
• Wrong formats
• Noise
• Inconsistent naming

Before training any AI or Machine Learning model, data must be cleaned and prepared.

This process is called:

Data Cleaning and Preprocessing.

Data preprocessing is one of the most important stages in Data Science.

In real-world AI projects, data scientists spend:

• 70–80% of their time cleaning data
• Only 20–30% building models

Even the most advanced AI algorithm will fail if the dataset is poor.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS DATA CLEANING?

-----------------------------------

Data cleaning means:

Identifying and fixing problems in datasets.

This includes:

• Removing errors
• Filling missing values
• Standardizing formats
• Removing duplicates
• Correcting inconsistent data

-----------------------------------

# WHY DATA CLEANING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Machine Learning models learn patterns from data.

If the data is:

• Incorrect
• Incomplete
• Noisy

then the AI model learns incorrect patterns.

This leads to:

• Poor accuracy
• Wrong predictions
• Biased results
• Unstable models

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES OF DIRTY DATA

-----------------------------------

Example dataset:

| Name | Age | Salary |
|------|------|---------|
| John | 25 | 50000 |
| Alice | NaN | 60000 |
| John | 25 | 50000 |
| Bob | twenty | NaN |

Problems:

• Missing values
• Duplicate rows
• Invalid data type

-----------------------------------

# IMPORTING PANDAS

-----------------------------------

Before preprocessing, import Pandas.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

import pandas as pd

-----------------------------------

# LOADING A DATASET

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# LOAD CSV FILE

-----------------------------------

df = pd.read_csv('students.csv')

-----------------------------------

# VIEWING THE DATASET

-----------------------------------

Always inspect the dataset first.

-----------------------------------

# head()

-----------------------------------

Shows first rows.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df.head())

-----------------------------------

# info()

-----------------------------------

Provides dataset summary.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df.info())

-----------------------------------

# WHAT info() REVEALS

-----------------------------------

• Missing values
• Data types
• Column names
• Total entries

-----------------------------------

# UNDERSTANDING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Missing values are empty or unavailable data.

In Pandas, missing values are represented as:

NaN

Meaning:

Not a Number.

-----------------------------------

# WHY MISSING VALUES ARE DANGEROUS

-----------------------------------

Missing data can:

• Break ML models
• Reduce accuracy
• Cause incorrect analysis

-----------------------------------

# CHECKING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Pandas provides:

isnull()

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df.isnull())

-----------------------------------

# COUNTING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(df.isnull().sum())

-----------------------------------

# OUTPUT EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Name      0
Age       2
Salary    1

-----------------------------------

# REMOVING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Missing rows can be removed.

-----------------------------------

# dropna()

-----------------------------------

Removes rows containing NaN values.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

cleaned = df.dropna()

-----------------------------------

# WHEN TO USE dropna()

-----------------------------------

Use when:

• Very few missing values exist
• Missing rows are not important

-----------------------------------

# FILLING MISSING VALUES

-----------------------------------

Instead of removing data, we can fill missing values.

-----------------------------------

# fillna()

-----------------------------------

Used to replace NaN values.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

filled = df.fillna(0)

-----------------------------------

# FILLING WITH MEAN

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# FILL AGE WITH AVERAGE

-----------------------------------

df['Age'] = df['Age'].fillna(df['Age'].mean())

-----------------------------------

# WHY FILLING WITH MEAN IS COMMON

-----------------------------------

It preserves dataset size while reducing missing data impact.

-----------------------------------

# FILLING WITH MODE

-----------------------------------

Mode = Most frequent value.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

df['City'] = df['City'].fillna(df['City'].mode()[0])

-----------------------------------

# DROPPING COLUMNS

-----------------------------------

Sometimes entire columns are unnecessary.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

df = df.drop('TemporaryColumn', axis=1)

-----------------------------------

# RENAMING COLUMNS

-----------------------------------

Datasets often contain confusing column names.

-----------------------------------

# rename()

-----------------------------------

Used to rename columns.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

df = df.rename(columns={
    'fname': 'FirstName',
    'lname': 'LastName'
})

-----------------------------------

# WHY RENAMING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Improves:

• Readability
• Analysis clarity
• Team collaboration

-----------------------------------

# DATA TYPE CONVERSION

-----------------------------------

Sometimes data types are incorrect.

Example:

Age stored as text instead of integer.

-----------------------------------

# CHECKING DATA TYPES

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(df.dtypes)

-----------------------------------

# astype()

-----------------------------------

Used to convert data types.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

df['Age'] = df['Age'].astype(int)

-----------------------------------

# COMMON DATA TYPES

-----------------------------------

• int → Integer
• float → Decimal numbers
• object → Strings/text
• bool → True/False

-----------------------------------

# WHY DATA TYPES MATTER

-----------------------------------

Machine Learning models require proper numerical formats.

Incorrect types can:

• Cause errors
• Slow computations
• Produce incorrect results

-----------------------------------

# DUPLICATE DATA

-----------------------------------

Duplicate rows are repeated entries.

-----------------------------------

# WHY DUPLICATES ARE BAD

-----------------------------------

Duplicates can:

• Bias ML models
• Distort statistics
• Increase dataset size unnecessarily

-----------------------------------

# CHECKING DUPLICATES

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(df.duplicated())

-----------------------------------

# COUNTING DUPLICATES

-----------------------------------

Example:

print(df.duplicated().sum())

-----------------------------------

# REMOVING DUPLICATES

-----------------------------------

Use:

-----------------------------------

# drop_duplicates()

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# REMOVE DUPLICATES

-----------------------------------

df = df.drop_duplicates()

-----------------------------------

# STRING CLEANING

-----------------------------------

Text data often contains:

• Extra spaces
• Inconsistent capitalization
• Typing errors

-----------------------------------

# REMOVING EXTRA SPACES

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# REMOVE SPACES

-----------------------------------

df['Name'] = df['Name'].str.strip()

-----------------------------------

# CONVERTING TO LOWERCASE

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# LOWERCASE

-----------------------------------

df['City'] = df['City'].str.lower()

-----------------------------------

# FILTERING INVALID DATA

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# KEEP ONLY POSITIVE AGES

-----------------------------------

df = df[df['Age'] > 0]

-----------------------------------

# OUTLIERS IN DATA

-----------------------------------

Outliers are abnormal values.

Example:

Salary = 999999999

-----------------------------------

# WHY OUTLIERS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Outliers can:

• Distort averages
• Mislead AI models
• Reduce prediction quality

-----------------------------------

# BASIC OUTLIER FILTERING

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# REMOVE LARGE VALUES

-----------------------------------

df = df[df['Salary'] < 100000]

-----------------------------------

# FEATURE ENGINEERING

-----------------------------------

Creating new useful columns from existing data.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

-----------------------------------

# CREATE PASS COLUMN

-----------------------------------

df['Pass'] = df['Marks'] > 40

-----------------------------------

# WHY FEATURE ENGINEERING MATTERS

-----------------------------------

Better features improve Machine Learning performance.

-----------------------------------

# SAVING CLEANED DATA

-----------------------------------

After preprocessing, datasets are saved.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

df.to_csv('cleaned_data.csv', index=False)

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD AI WORKFLOW

-----------------------------------

Raw Data → Cleaning → Preprocessing → Feature Engineering → ML Training

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Deleting too much data

-----------------------------------

2. Ignoring missing values

-----------------------------------

3. Using wrong data types

-----------------------------------

4. Forgetting to remove duplicates

-----------------------------------

5. Accidentally overwriting original datasets

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Always inspect datasets before cleaning

-----------------------------------

2. Keep backup of original data

-----------------------------------

3. Handle missing values carefully

-----------------------------------

4. Validate data types

-----------------------------------

5. Document every transformation step

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

Data preprocessing is used in:

• Medical AI systems
• Fraud detection
• Recommendation engines
• Chatbots
• Face recognition
• Business analytics
• Autonomous vehicles

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What data cleaning is
• Why preprocessing is critical in AI
• Handling missing values
• isnull(), fillna(), dropna()
• Renaming columns
• Converting data types
• Removing duplicates
• String cleaning
• Filtering invalid data
• Basic outlier handling
• Feature engineering
• Saving cleaned datasets
• Real-world preprocessing workflows

Data cleaning and preprocessing are among the most important skills in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science. Clean, structured, and reliable data is the foundation of every successful AI system.
keyPoints: Data cleaning is essential before Machine Learning
Missing values can reduce model accuracy
isnull() detects missing values in datasets
fillna() replaces missing values with custom values
dropna() removes rows containing missing values
rename() improves column readability
astype() converts data types
drop_duplicates() removes repeated records
Feature engineering improves AI model performance
Proper preprocessing creates reliable datasets
Real-world datasets are often messy and inconsistent
Clean data leads to better AI predictions
content.practicalTask: Load a messy dataset using Pandas, identify missing values, remove duplicates, rename columns, convert incorrect data types, and generate a cleaned dataset ready for Machine Learning.
content.assignment: Clean a provided dirty dataset and document every preprocessing transformation including missing value handling, duplicate removal, type conversion, and feature engineering.
content.miniProject: Build a Data Cleaning Pipeline using Pandas that automatically detects missing values, removes duplicates, standardizes column names, converts data types, filters invalid data, and exports a fully cleaned dataset.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Filtering, Grouping & Aggregation in Pandas**

In Data Science and Artificial Intelligence, simply loading a dataset is not enough.

The real power comes from:

• Filtering useful data
• Grouping records
• Finding patterns
• Combining datasets
• Calculating statistics
• Extracting insights

This process is called:

Data Analysis.

Pandas provides extremely powerful tools for analyzing structured datasets efficiently.

These techniques are heavily used in:

• AI systems
• Business intelligence
• Sales analytics
• Recommendation engines
• Healthcare analytics
• Fraud detection
• Machine Learning preprocessing

-----------------------------------

# WHY DATA ANALYSIS IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Machine Learning models depend on understanding the dataset before training.

Data analysis helps answer questions like:

• Which product sells the most?
• Which region generates highest revenue?
• Which students perform best?
• Which users are inactive?
• Which features are important?

-----------------------------------

# IMPORTING PANDAS

-----------------------------------

Before analysis, import Pandas.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

import pandas as pd

-----------------------------------

# LOADING A DATASET

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# LOAD CSV FILE

-----------------------------------

df = pd.read_csv('sales.csv')

-----------------------------------

# VIEWING DATA

-----------------------------------

Always inspect the dataset first.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df.head())

-----------------------------------

# FILTERING DATA

-----------------------------------

Filtering means selecting only the rows that match specific conditions.

-----------------------------------

# WHY FILTERING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Filtering helps:

• Focus on useful records
• Remove unnecessary data
• Analyze subsets
• Build ML datasets

-----------------------------------

# BOOLEAN FILTERING

-----------------------------------

Boolean filtering uses conditions.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

high_sales = df[df['Sales'] > 5000]

print(high_sales)

-----------------------------------

# HOW IT WORKS

-----------------------------------

Condition:

Sales > 5000

Only matching rows are returned.

-----------------------------------

# MULTIPLE CONDITIONS

-----------------------------------

Use:

• & for AND
• | for OR

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

filtered = df[
    (df['Sales'] > 5000) &
    (df['Region'] == 'North')
]

print(filtered)

-----------------------------------

# FILTERING TEXT DATA

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# FILTER BY CITY

-----------------------------------

city_data = df[df['City'] == 'Delhi']

-----------------------------------

# isin()

-----------------------------------

Used for multiple values.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

regions = df[df['Region'].isin(['North', 'South'])]

-----------------------------------

# SORTING DATA

-----------------------------------

Sorting organizes datasets.

-----------------------------------

# sort_values()

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# SORT BY SALES

-----------------------------------

sorted_df = df.sort_values(by='Sales')

-----------------------------------

# DESCENDING ORDER

-----------------------------------

Example:

sorted_df = df.sort_values(
    by='Sales',
    ascending=False
)

-----------------------------------

# GROUPING DATA

-----------------------------------

Grouping is one of the most powerful features in Pandas.

It allows us to:

• Divide data into categories
• Calculate summaries
• Compare groups

-----------------------------------

# groupby()

-----------------------------------

Used to group rows by category.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

region_group = df.groupby('Region')

-----------------------------------

# CALCULATING GROUP TOTALS

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# TOTAL SALES BY REGION

-----------------------------------

print(df.groupby('Region')['Sales'].sum())

-----------------------------------

# OUTPUT EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

North    50000
South    45000
East     38000
West     42000

-----------------------------------

# WHY GROUPING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used in:

• Sales analysis
• Customer analytics
• AI predictions
• Fraud detection
• Business reports

-----------------------------------

# MULTIPLE GROUPING

-----------------------------------

Data can be grouped using multiple columns.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(
    df.groupby(['Region', 'Product'])['Sales'].sum()
)

-----------------------------------

# AGGREGATION FUNCTIONS

-----------------------------------

Aggregation means calculating summary statistics.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON AGGREGATION FUNCTIONS

-----------------------------------

• sum()
• mean()
• min()
• max()
• count()
• std()

-----------------------------------

# mean()

-----------------------------------

Calculates average.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df.groupby('Region')['Sales'].mean())

-----------------------------------

# count()

-----------------------------------

Counts records.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

print(df.groupby('Region')['Sales'].count())

-----------------------------------

# agg()

-----------------------------------

Performs multiple aggregations together.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

summary = df.groupby('Region')['Sales'].agg([
    'sum',
    'mean',
    'max'
])

print(summary)

-----------------------------------

# WHY agg() IS POWERFUL

-----------------------------------

It creates complete summaries efficiently.

-----------------------------------

# transform()

-----------------------------------

transform() returns transformed values while keeping original dataset shape.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

-----------------------------------

# NORMALIZE SALES BY REGION

-----------------------------------

df['AvgSales'] = df.groupby('Region')['Sales'].transform('mean')

-----------------------------------

# DIFFERENCE BETWEEN agg() AND transform()

-----------------------------------

agg():

Returns summarized results.

transform():

Returns transformed values matching original rows.

-----------------------------------

# PIVOT TABLES

-----------------------------------

Pivot tables summarize large datasets.

-----------------------------------

# WHY PIVOT TABLES ARE USEFUL

-----------------------------------

They help:

• Compare categories
• Analyze trends
• Generate reports

-----------------------------------

# pivot_table()

-----------------------------------

Creates summarized tables.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

pivot = pd.pivot_table(
    df,
    values='Sales',
    index='Region',
    columns='Product',
    aggfunc='sum'
)

print(pivot)

-----------------------------------

# OUTPUT EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

| Region | Laptop | Phone |
|--------|---------|--------|
| North | 20000 | 30000 |
| South | 15000 | 30000 |

-----------------------------------

# MERGING DATASETS

-----------------------------------

Real-world AI projects often use multiple datasets.

Example:

• Customer dataset
• Orders dataset
• Payment dataset

These datasets must be combined.

-----------------------------------

# merge()

-----------------------------------

Used to combine datasets.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

customers = pd.read_csv('customers.csv')
orders = pd.read_csv('orders.csv')

merged = pd.merge(
    customers,
    orders,
    on='CustomerID'
)

print(merged)

-----------------------------------

# TYPES OF MERGE

-----------------------------------

1. Inner Join

2. Left Join

3. Right Join

4. Outer Join

-----------------------------------

# INNER JOIN

-----------------------------------

Returns matching records only.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

merged = pd.merge(
    df1,
    df2,
    how='inner'
)

-----------------------------------

# LEFT JOIN

-----------------------------------

Keeps all rows from left dataset.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

merged = pd.merge(
    df1,
    df2,
    how='left'
)

-----------------------------------

# RIGHT JOIN

-----------------------------------

Keeps all rows from right dataset.

-----------------------------------

# OUTER JOIN

-----------------------------------

Keeps all rows from both datasets.

-----------------------------------

# WHY JOINS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

AI systems often combine data from:

• Multiple databases
• APIs
• CSV files
• External systems

-----------------------------------

# CONCATENATING DATASETS

-----------------------------------

concat() combines datasets vertically or horizontally.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

combined = pd.concat([df1, df2])

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD ANALYSIS EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Sales Dataset Analysis:

Questions:

• Which region has highest sales?
• Which product sells most?
• Average sales per region?
• Which customers buy most frequently?

These insights help businesses make decisions.

-----------------------------------

# GROUPING IN MACHINE LEARNING

-----------------------------------

Grouping helps:

• Analyze target classes
• Understand patterns
• Detect imbalance
• Build features

-----------------------------------

# FEATURE ENGINEERING USING GROUPBY

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# AVG SALES PER REGION

-----------------------------------

df['RegionAvg'] = df.groupby('Region')['Sales'].transform('mean')

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Forgetting parentheses in conditions

-----------------------------------

2. Using wrong column names

-----------------------------------

3. Confusing agg() and transform()

-----------------------------------

4. Incorrect join keys in merge()

-----------------------------------

5. Ignoring missing values before grouping

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Always inspect data before grouping

-----------------------------------

2. Use meaningful aggregations

-----------------------------------

3. Handle missing values before analysis

-----------------------------------

4. Validate merged datasets carefully

-----------------------------------

5. Use pivot tables for reporting

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

Filtering and grouping are used in:

• Sales dashboards
• Fraud detection systems
• AI recommendation engines
• Financial analytics
• Healthcare analysis
• Customer segmentation
• Marketing analytics

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• Filtering datasets using conditions
• Boolean filtering
• Sorting data
• groupby() operations
• Aggregation functions
• agg() and transform()
• Pivot tables
• merge() and joins
• Combining datasets
• Grouped analysis workflows
• Real-world business analytics
• AI preprocessing techniques

Filtering, grouping, and aggregation are among the most powerful skills in Data Science and Artificial Intelligence. These techniques allow data scientists to transform raw datasets into meaningful insights that drive Machine Learning and business decisions.
keyPoints: Filtering selects rows based on conditions
Boolean filtering is widely used in data analysis
groupby() helps categorize and summarize datasets
Aggregation functions calculate statistical summaries
agg() performs multiple aggregations together
transform() modifies grouped data while preserving structure
Pivot tables summarize large datasets efficiently
merge() combines multiple datasets together
Inner, left, right, and outer joins serve different purposes
Filtering and grouping are critical for AI preprocessing
Data analysis helps uncover patterns and trends
Grouped insights improve business and AI decision-making
content.practicalTask: Load a sales dataset using Pandas, filter records by region, group data using groupby(), calculate total and average sales, create pivot tables, and merge related datasets together.
content.assignment: Perform grouped analysis on a provided dataset by filtering rows, applying aggregation functions, generating pivot tables, and merging datasets to create meaningful business insights.
content.miniProject: Build a Sales Analytics Dashboard Backend using Pandas that filters sales records, groups data by categories, calculates revenue summaries, creates pivot reports, and merges customer and product datasets for advanced business analysis.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Data Visualization in AI & Data Science**

Data Visualization is the process of representing data graphically using charts, graphs, and plots.

In Artificial Intelligence and Data Science, visualization is extremely important because humans understand visual information much faster than raw tables.

A dataset containing thousands of rows may look confusing in table format, but a graph can instantly reveal:

• Trends
• Patterns
• Correlations
• Outliers
• Relationships
• Distributions

Visualization helps data scientists and AI engineers:

• Understand datasets better
• Detect problems in data
• Find hidden insights
• Present findings clearly
• Improve Machine Learning decisions

-----------------------------------

# WHY DATA VISUALIZATION IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Without visualization, analyzing large datasets becomes difficult.

Graphs help answer questions like:

• Which product sells the most?
• Is revenue increasing over time?
• Which variables are related?
• Are there unusual values?
• Which feature affects predictions most?

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

Visualization is used in:

• AI dashboards
• Stock market analysis
• Healthcare analytics
• Fraud detection
• Business intelligence
• Weather forecasting
• Recommendation systems
• Machine Learning model analysis

-----------------------------------

# LIBRARIES USED FOR VISUALIZATION

-----------------------------------

Two major Python libraries:

1. Matplotlib

2. Seaborn

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS MATPLOTLIB?

-----------------------------------

Matplotlib is the most popular Python plotting library.

It provides:

• Basic charts
• Full customization
• Professional visualizations

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS SEABORN?

-----------------------------------

Seaborn is built on top of Matplotlib.

It provides:

• Beautiful visualizations
• Better styling
• Statistical plots
• Simpler syntax

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLATION

-----------------------------------

Install both libraries using pip.

-----------------------------------

# INSTALL COMMAND

-----------------------------------

pip install matplotlib seaborn

-----------------------------------

# IMPORTING LIBRARIES

-----------------------------------

Example:

-----------------------------------

# IMPORT VISUALIZATION LIBRARIES

-----------------------------------

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

-----------------------------------

# BASIC LINE PLOT

-----------------------------------

Line plots show trends over time.

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

-----------------------------------

• Stock prices
• Temperature changes
• Revenue growth
• AI model accuracy over epochs

-----------------------------------

# LINE PLOT EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
y = [10, 20, 25, 30, 40]

plt.plot(x, y)
plt.show()

-----------------------------------

# HOW plt.plot() WORKS

-----------------------------------

x-axis:

Represents categories or time.

y-axis:

Represents values.

-----------------------------------

# ADDING TITLES AND LABELS

-----------------------------------

Charts should always contain:

• Title
• X-axis label
• Y-axis label

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

plt.plot(x, y)
plt.title('Sales Growth')
plt.xlabel('Months')
plt.ylabel('Revenue')
plt.show()

-----------------------------------

# BAR CHARTS

-----------------------------------

Bar charts compare categories.

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

-----------------------------------

• Product sales
• Student marks
• Population comparison
• Region-wise revenue

-----------------------------------

# BAR CHART EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

products = ['Laptop', 'Phone', 'Tablet']
sales = [120, 300, 150]

plt.bar(products, sales)
plt.show()

-----------------------------------

# WHY BAR CHARTS ARE USEFUL

-----------------------------------

They make comparisons easier.

-----------------------------------

# SCATTER PLOTS

-----------------------------------

Scatter plots show relationships between two variables.

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

-----------------------------------

• Height vs weight
• Study hours vs marks
• Advertising vs sales

-----------------------------------

# SCATTER PLOT EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
y = [2, 4, 5, 4, 5]

plt.scatter(x, y)
plt.show()

-----------------------------------

# WHY SCATTER PLOTS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

They help detect:

• Correlations
• Clusters
• Outliers

-----------------------------------

# HISTOGRAMS

-----------------------------------

Histograms show data distribution.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS DISTRIBUTION?

-----------------------------------

Distribution shows:

• How values are spread
• Which values occur most frequently

-----------------------------------

# HISTOGRAM EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

marks = [50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 95, 100]

plt.hist(marks)
plt.show()

-----------------------------------

# WHY HISTOGRAMS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used in:

• AI preprocessing
• Data analysis
• Detecting skewed data
• Understanding feature distributions

-----------------------------------

# PIE CHARTS

-----------------------------------

Pie charts show proportions.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

labels = ['Python', 'Java', 'C++']
sizes = [50, 30, 20]

plt.pie(sizes, labels=labels)
plt.show()

-----------------------------------

# SEABORN VISUALIZATIONS

-----------------------------------

Seaborn creates more attractive charts.

-----------------------------------

# SEABORN STYLE

-----------------------------------

Example:

sns.set_style('darkgrid')

-----------------------------------

# HEATMAPS

-----------------------------------

Heatmaps visualize correlations between variables.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS CORRELATION?

-----------------------------------

Correlation measures relationship strength between variables.

-----------------------------------

# CORRELATION FORMULA

-----------------------------------



-----------------------------------

# CORRELATION MEANING

-----------------------------------

r = 1:

Strong positive relationship.

r = -1:

Strong negative relationship.

r = 0:

No relationship.

-----------------------------------

# HEATMAP EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

corr = df.corr()

sns.heatmap(corr)
plt.show()

-----------------------------------

# WHY HEATMAPS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Used in Machine Learning for:

• Feature selection
• Identifying relationships
• Removing redundant features

-----------------------------------

# PAIR PLOTS

-----------------------------------

Pair plots compare multiple variables together.

-----------------------------------

# pairplot() EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

sns.pairplot(df)
plt.show()

-----------------------------------

# WHAT PAIR PLOTS SHOW

-----------------------------------

• Relationships between features
• Distributions
• Clusters
• Patterns

-----------------------------------

# STYLING CHARTS

-----------------------------------

Good visualization should be:

• Clear
• Readable
• Attractive
• Informative

-----------------------------------

# CHANGING COLORS

-----------------------------------

Example:

plt.plot(x, y, color='red')

-----------------------------------

# CHANGING FIGURE SIZE

-----------------------------------

Example:

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))

-----------------------------------

# ADDING GRID

-----------------------------------

Example:

plt.grid(True)

-----------------------------------

# LEGENDS

-----------------------------------

Legends explain plotted data.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

plt.plot(x, y, label='Sales')
plt.legend()

-----------------------------------

# MULTIPLE PLOTS

-----------------------------------

Multiple graphs can appear together.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
plt.plot(x, y)

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
plt.bar(products, sales)

plt.show()

-----------------------------------

# SAVING CHARTS

-----------------------------------

Charts can be saved as images.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

plt.savefig('chart.png')

-----------------------------------

# DATA VISUALIZATION IN MACHINE LEARNING

-----------------------------------

Visualization helps ML engineers:

• Understand datasets
• Detect imbalance
• Analyze features
• Identify outliers
• Compare model performance

-----------------------------------

# MODEL PERFORMANCE VISUALIZATION

-----------------------------------

Example:

• Accuracy graphs
• Loss curves
• Confusion matrices

-----------------------------------

# OUTLIER DETECTION USING VISUALIZATION

-----------------------------------

Scatter plots and box plots help identify abnormal values.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Forgetting labels and titles

-----------------------------------

2. Using incorrect chart types

-----------------------------------

3. Overcrowding graphs

-----------------------------------

4. Ignoring axis scaling

-----------------------------------

5. Using too many colors unnecessarily
