-----------------------------------

# WHAT request.json DOES

-----------------------------------

It reads:

Incoming JSON data.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT jsonify() DOES

-----------------------------------

Converts Python dictionaries into:

JSON responses.

-----------------------------------

# RUNNING THE FLASK SERVER

-----------------------------------

app.run(debug=True)

-----------------------------------

# FLASK SERVER ADDRESS

-----------------------------------

http://127.0.0.1:5000

-----------------------------------

# TESTING APIS

-----------------------------------

APIs are tested using:

• Postman
• Browser
• Python requests
• Thunder Client

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS POSTMAN?

-----------------------------------

Postman is:

A professional API testing tool.

-----------------------------------

# TESTING WITH POSTMAN

-----------------------------------

Steps:

1. Select POST method

2. Enter API URL

3. Add JSON body

4. Click Send

5. View prediction response

-----------------------------------

# POSTMAN JSON BODY

-----------------------------------

{
    "age": 30,
    "glucose": 120
}

-----------------------------------

# WHY API TESTING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Testing verifies:

• Input handling
• Prediction accuracy
• Error handling
• API functionality

-----------------------------------

# INPUT VALIDATION

-----------------------------------

APIs should validate:

User input.

-----------------------------------

# WHY VALIDATION MATTERS

-----------------------------------

Prevents:

• Crashes
• Invalid predictions
• Security issues

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE VALIDATION

-----------------------------------

if 'age' not in data:
    return jsonify({
        'error':'Age missing'
    })

-----------------------------------

# HANDLING INVALID INPUTS

-----------------------------------

Good APIs handle:

• Missing fields
• Wrong data types
• Invalid values

-----------------------------------

# ERROR HANDLING EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

try:
    prediction = model.predict(input_data)

except Exception as e:
    return jsonify({
        'error': str(e)
    })

-----------------------------------

# MULTIPLE FEATURES

-----------------------------------

Real ML models often use:

Multiple inputs.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

input_data = [[
    age,
    glucose,
    bmi,
    blood_pressure
]]

-----------------------------------

# WHY FEATURE ORDER MATTERS

-----------------------------------

The model expects:

Features in the same order used during training.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON DEPLOYMENT WORKFLOW

-----------------------------------

1. Train model

2. Save model

3. Build Flask API

4. Load model

5. Accept requests

6. Generate predictions

7. Return results

-----------------------------------

# AI SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE

-----------------------------------

Frontend → Flask API → ML Model → Database

-----------------------------------

# DEPLOYING AI APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

Flask APIs can later be deployed on:

• Render
• Railway
• AWS
• Heroku
• Google Cloud
• Azure

-----------------------------------

# WHY REAL-TIME PREDICTIONS MATTER

-----------------------------------

Real-world AI systems require:

Instant responses.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLES OF REAL-TIME AI

-----------------------------------

• Face recognition
• Spam filtering
• Fraud detection
• Recommendation systems
• Chatbots

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Forgetting to load the model

-----------------------------------

2. Incorrect feature order

-----------------------------------

3. Using wrong request methods

-----------------------------------

4. Forgetting input validation

-----------------------------------

5. Returning non-JSON responses

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Validate all user inputs

-----------------------------------

2. Keep models separate from routes

-----------------------------------

3. Use JSON APIs for ML systems

-----------------------------------

4. Handle exceptions properly

-----------------------------------

5. Test APIs using Postman

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What model serving is
• Model serialization concepts
• Saving ML models using Pickle
• Saving ML models using Joblib
• Loading models inside Flask
• Building prediction APIs
• JSON request and response handling
• API testing with Postman
• Input validation and error handling
• Real-world ML deployment workflow

Serving ML models is one of the most important skills in AI engineering because it transforms Machine Learning models from research projects into real-world intelligent systems. Modern AI applications such as recommendation engines, medical diagnosis systems, fraud detection platforms, chatbots, and computer vision systems all rely on APIs and deployment frameworks like Flask to provide predictions to users in real time.
keyPoints: Model serving enables real-time ML predictions
Serialization converts models into storable files
Pickle and Joblib are used for saving ML models
Flask APIs can load and serve trained models
POST requests are commonly used for prediction APIs
request.json accesses incoming JSON input
jsonify() returns JSON responses
Input validation prevents invalid predictions
Postman is used for API testing
ML APIs connect users with AI systems
Feature order must match training data format
Flask deployment is widely used in real-world AI applications
content.practicalTask: Train a diabetes prediction model using Scikit-learn, save it using Pickle or Joblib, load it inside a Flask application, and create a /predict API endpoint that returns prediction results in JSON format.
content.assignment: Build a Flask-based Machine Learning API with proper input validation, exception handling, JSON request support, and prediction responses. Test the API using Postman with multiple sample inputs.
content.miniProject: AI Health Prediction API using Flask and Scikit-learn with model serialization, REST API endpoints, JSON input handling, prediction generation, input validation, and Postman-tested deployment workflow.

content.theory:
**Streamlit for Interactive ML Apps**

In previous deployment sessions, you learned:

• Flask basics
• Building APIs
• Serving ML models
• Prediction endpoints
• JSON responses
• API testing using Postman

Today, you will learn another extremely powerful AI deployment framework:

Streamlit.

Streamlit allows developers to:

Convert Python scripts into interactive web applications.

The best part is:

You can build beautiful AI applications using only Python.

No frontend knowledge is required.

Streamlit is widely used for:

• AI dashboards
• Data science applications
• Machine Learning demos
• Interactive analytics tools
• Deep Learning applications
• NLP systems
• Computer Vision applications

In this session, you will learn:

• What Streamlit is
• Why Streamlit is popular
• Streamlit app structure
• User interface widgets
• Displaying data and charts
• Connecting ML models
• Deploying Streamlit applications

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

• Build interactive AI applications
• Create ML prediction dashboards
• Build data visualization apps
• Deploy ML models with user-friendly interfaces

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS STREAMLIT?

-----------------------------------

Streamlit is:

A Python framework for building interactive web applications.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE DEFINITION

-----------------------------------

Streamlit converts:

Python scripts into web apps.

-----------------------------------

# WHY STREAMLIT IS POPULAR

-----------------------------------

Streamlit is:

• Beginner-friendly
• Fast to build
• Interactive
• Lightweight
• Perfect for AI projects

-----------------------------------

# WHY DATA SCIENTISTS LOVE STREAMLIT

-----------------------------------

No frontend development knowledge is required.

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT VS FLASK

-----------------------------------

| Flask | Streamlit |
|-------|------------|
| Backend framework | Interactive frontend framework |
| Requires HTML/CSS for UI | Built-in UI components |
| Better for APIs | Better for dashboards |
| More flexible | Faster development |

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD STREAMLIT APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

Streamlit is used for:

• AI dashboards
• Data analytics platforms
• Chatbot interfaces
• Recommendation systems
• OCR applications
• ML model demos
• Stock prediction apps

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING STREAMLIT

-----------------------------------

Install Streamlit using:

pip install streamlit

-----------------------------------

# IMPORTING STREAMLIT

-----------------------------------

import streamlit as st

-----------------------------------

# WHY 'st' IS USED

-----------------------------------

'st' is:

The standard alias for Streamlit.

-----------------------------------

# FIRST STREAMLIT APP

-----------------------------------

import streamlit as st

st.title('My First AI App')

-----------------------------------

# RUNNING A STREAMLIT APP

-----------------------------------

streamlit run app.py

-----------------------------------

# WHAT HAPPENS AFTER RUNNING

-----------------------------------

Streamlit launches:

A local web application.

-----------------------------------

# DEFAULT STREAMLIT ADDRESS

-----------------------------------

http://localhost:8501

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS st.title()?

-----------------------------------

st.title() displays:

Large page headings.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

st.title('AI Prediction System')

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS st.write()?

-----------------------------------

st.write() displays:

Text, variables, data, and outputs.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

st.write('Welcome to Machine Learning')

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT USER INTERFACE

-----------------------------------

Streamlit provides:

Interactive UI components.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT ARE WIDGETS?

-----------------------------------

Widgets allow users to:

Interact with applications.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON STREAMLIT WIDGETS

-----------------------------------

• Buttons
• Sliders
• Text inputs
• Dropdown menus
• Checkboxes
• File uploaders

-----------------------------------

# TEXT INPUT WIDGET

-----------------------------------

name = st.text_input('Enter your name')

-----------------------------------

# WHAT THIS DOES

-----------------------------------

Allows users to:

Type text.

-----------------------------------

# NUMBER INPUT WIDGET

-----------------------------------

age = st.number_input('Enter Age')

-----------------------------------

# SLIDER WIDGET

-----------------------------------

salary = st.slider(
    'Select Salary',
    10000,
    100000
)

-----------------------------------

# WHY SLIDERS ARE USEFUL

-----------------------------------

They simplify:

Numeric input selection.

-----------------------------------

# BUTTON WIDGET

-----------------------------------

if st.button('Predict'):
    st.write('Prediction Generated')

-----------------------------------

# WHAT BUTTONS DO

-----------------------------------

Buttons trigger:

Actions inside the app.

-----------------------------------

# DROPDOWN MENUS

-----------------------------------

option = st.selectbox(
    'Choose Model',
    ['Linear Regression', 'Random Forest']
)

-----------------------------------

# CHECKBOX WIDGET

-----------------------------------

agree = st.checkbox('I Agree')

-----------------------------------

# RADIO BUTTONS

-----------------------------------

gender = st.radio(
    'Select Gender',
    ['Male', 'Female']
)

-----------------------------------

# FILE UPLOAD WIDGET

-----------------------------------

uploaded_file = st.file_uploader(
    'Upload CSV File'
)

-----------------------------------

# WHY FILE UPLOADS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Users can upload:

Datasets or images.

-----------------------------------

# DISPLAYING DATAFRAMES

-----------------------------------

Streamlit integrates easily with:

Pandas.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

import pandas as pd

-----------------------------------

# DISPLAYING A DATAFRAME

-----------------------------------

st.dataframe(df)

-----------------------------------

# WHAT st.dataframe() DOES

-----------------------------------

Displays:

Interactive tables.

-----------------------------------

# DISPLAYING TABLES

-----------------------------------

st.table(df)

-----------------------------------

# DATAFRAME VS TABLE

-----------------------------------

| st.dataframe() | st.table() |
|----------------|------------|
| Interactive | Static |
| Scrollable | Fixed |
| Better for large data | Better for small data |

-----------------------------------

# DISPLAYING METRICS

-----------------------------------

st.metric('Accuracy', '95%')

-----------------------------------

# WHY METRICS ARE USEFUL

-----------------------------------

Metrics highlight:

Important values.

-----------------------------------

# DISPLAYING IMAGES

-----------------------------------

st.image('image.jpg')

-----------------------------------

# DISPLAYING CHARTS

-----------------------------------

Streamlit supports:

• Matplotlib
• Plotly
• Altair
• Seaborn

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE LINE CHART

-----------------------------------

st.line_chart(data)

-----------------------------------

# BAR CHART

-----------------------------------

st.bar_chart(data)

-----------------------------------

# AREA CHART

-----------------------------------

st.area_chart(data)

-----------------------------------

# WHY VISUALIZATIONS MATTER

-----------------------------------

Visualizations help users:

Understand data easily.

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT + MACHINE LEARNING

-----------------------------------

Streamlit can integrate directly with:

ML models.

-----------------------------------

# LOADING A MODEL

-----------------------------------

import pickle

model = pickle.load(
    open('model.pkl', 'rb')
)

-----------------------------------

# TAKING USER INPUT

-----------------------------------

age = st.number_input('Age')

glucose = st.number_input('Glucose Level')

-----------------------------------

# GENERATING PREDICTIONS

-----------------------------------

if st.button('Predict'):

    prediction = model.predict(
        [[age, glucose]]
    )

    st.write(prediction)

-----------------------------------

# COMPLETE STREAMLIT ML FLOW

-----------------------------------

User Input → Streamlit UI → ML Model → Prediction → Output Display

-----------------------------------

# WHY STREAMLIT IS GREAT FOR AI DEMOS

-----------------------------------

Streamlit quickly converts:

ML projects into usable applications.

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT LAYOUTS

-----------------------------------

Streamlit supports:

• Columns
• Sidebars
• Expanders
• Tabs

-----------------------------------

# USING SIDEBARS

-----------------------------------

st.sidebar.title('Menu')

-----------------------------------

# WHY SIDEBARS ARE USEFUL

-----------------------------------

They organize:

Navigation and controls.

-----------------------------------

# COLUMNS IN STREAMLIT

-----------------------------------

col1, col2 = st.columns(2)

-----------------------------------

# WHY COLUMNS HELP

-----------------------------------

They improve:

UI organization.

-----------------------------------

# SPINNERS AND LOADERS

-----------------------------------

with st.spinner('Loading Model...'):
    model = load_model()

-----------------------------------

# WHY LOADERS MATTER

-----------------------------------

They improve:

User experience.

-----------------------------------

# SUCCESS AND ERROR MESSAGES

-----------------------------------

st.success('Prediction Completed')

st.error('Invalid Input')

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT SESSION STATE

-----------------------------------

Session state stores:

Temporary app data.

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT CLOUD DEPLOYMENT

-----------------------------------

Streamlit apps can be deployed on:

• Streamlit Cloud
• Render
• Railway
• AWS
• Heroku

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS STREAMLIT CLOUD?

-----------------------------------

A free hosting platform for:

Streamlit applications.

-----------------------------------

# DEPLOYMENT WORKFLOW

-----------------------------------

1. Build app

2. Push code to GitHub

3. Connect GitHub to Streamlit Cloud

4. Deploy application

-----------------------------------

# WHY DEPLOYMENT IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Deployment allows:

Real users to access AI systems.

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT FOR DATA SCIENCE

-----------------------------------

Data scientists use Streamlit for:

• Interactive reports
• EDA dashboards
• AI prototypes
• Model demonstrations

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT + COMPUTER VISION

-----------------------------------

Streamlit can display:

• Images
• Videos
• OCR outputs
• Detection results

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT + NLP

-----------------------------------

Streamlit can power:

• Chatbots
• Sentiment analysis tools
• Text summarizers

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Forgetting to rerun the app

-----------------------------------

2. Incorrect widget variable handling

-----------------------------------

3. Not validating user input

-----------------------------------

4. Loading models repeatedly inside buttons

-----------------------------------

5. Poor UI organization

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Keep interfaces simple

-----------------------------------

2. Use proper labels for widgets

-----------------------------------

3. Validate user input

-----------------------------------

4. Use charts for better visualization

-----------------------------------

5. Deploy projects professionally

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What Streamlit is
• Building Streamlit applications
• Streamlit widgets and UI components
• Text inputs and sliders
• Displaying dataframes and charts
• Integrating ML models with Streamlit
• Generating predictions interactively
• Streamlit layouts and sidebars
• Deployment using Streamlit Cloud
• Building interactive AI applications

Streamlit is one of the most powerful tools for AI engineers and data scientists because it enables rapid development of beautiful interactive Machine Learning applications entirely using Python. Modern AI dashboards, recommendation systems, NLP applications, and Computer Vision tools are frequently prototyped and deployed using Streamlit due to its simplicity, speed, and developer-friendly design.
keyPoints: Streamlit converts Python scripts into web applications
Streamlit is beginner-friendly and ideal for AI projects
Widgets allow users to interact with applications
st.title() and st.write() display content
Sliders and text inputs collect user data
st.dataframe() displays interactive tables
Streamlit supports charts and visualizations
ML models can be integrated directly into Streamlit apps
Buttons can trigger predictions and actions
Streamlit Cloud allows free deployment
Streamlit is widely used for AI dashboards and demos
Interactive ML applications improve user experience
content.practicalTask: Build a Streamlit application for a classification model that accepts user inputs through sliders and text fields, generates predictions using a trained ML model, and displays the results interactively.
content.assignment: Create a fully interactive Streamlit Machine Learning application with multiple user inputs, charts, prediction outputs, and proper UI organization. Deploy the application using Streamlit Cloud.
content.miniProject: Interactive AI Prediction Dashboard using Streamlit with ML model integration, interactive widgets, charts, dataframe visualization, prediction generation, and cloud deployment support.

content.theory:
**APIs & Integration**

In previous deployment sessions, you learned:

• Flask APIs
• ML model deployment
• Streamlit applications
• Prediction systems
• JSON handling

Today, you will learn one of the most important concepts in modern software and AI development:

APIs.

APIs power almost every modern application.

Whenever applications communicate with each other, APIs are usually involved.

Examples include:

• Weather apps
• ChatGPT integrations
• Payment gateways
• Google Maps
• Social media platforms
• AI systems
• Recommendation engines

In this session, you will learn:

• What APIs are
• REST API concepts
• HTTP requests
• GET and POST methods
• Using the requests library
• Handling JSON data
• API key management
• Integrating external AI APIs

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

• Consume external APIs
• Send requests to servers
• Process API responses
• Integrate AI services into applications
• Build API-powered AI systems

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS AN API?

-----------------------------------

API stands for:

Application Programming Interface.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE DEFINITION

-----------------------------------

An API allows:

Different applications to communicate.

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Weather App → Weather API → Weather Data

-----------------------------------

# WHY APIS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

APIs enable:

• Data sharing
• System integration
• Automation
• AI communication
• Cloud services

-----------------------------------

# MODERN APPLICATIONS USE APIS

-----------------------------------

Examples:

• Instagram API
• Google Maps API
• OpenAI API
• Stripe Payment API
• Weather APIs

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A REST API?

-----------------------------------

REST stands for:

Representational State Transfer.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE REST API EXPLANATION

-----------------------------------

REST APIs use:

HTTP requests for communication.

-----------------------------------

# WHY REST APIS ARE POPULAR

-----------------------------------

REST APIs are:

• Simple
• Fast
• Scalable
• Lightweight
• Easy to integrate

-----------------------------------

# CLIENT-SERVER ARCHITECTURE

-----------------------------------

REST APIs follow:

Client → Server communication.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A CLIENT?

-----------------------------------

The client is:

The application requesting data.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A SERVER?

-----------------------------------

The server:

Processes requests and returns responses.

-----------------------------------

# HOW APIS WORK

-----------------------------------

Client → Request → Server → Response

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS HTTP?

-----------------------------------

HTTP stands for:

HyperText Transfer Protocol.

-----------------------------------

# WHY HTTP MATTERS

-----------------------------------

HTTP enables:

Communication over the internet.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON HTTP METHODS

-----------------------------------

• GET
• POST
• PUT
• DELETE

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A GET REQUEST?

-----------------------------------

GET requests:

Retrieve data from servers.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Fetching weather information.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A POST REQUEST?

-----------------------------------

POST requests:

Send data to servers.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Sending chatbot prompts.

-----------------------------------

# GET VS POST

-----------------------------------

| GET | POST |
|-----|------|
| Retrieves data | Sends data |
| Data in URL | Data in body |
| Used for reading | Used for creating |
| More visible | More secure |

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS JSON?

-----------------------------------

JSON stands for:

JavaScript Object Notation.

-----------------------------------

# WHY JSON IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Most APIs exchange:

JSON data.

-----------------------------------

# JSON EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

{
    "name": "John",
    "age": 25
}

-----------------------------------

# WHY JSON IS POPULAR

-----------------------------------

JSON is:

• Lightweight
• Easy to read
• Human-friendly
• Machine-friendly

-----------------------------------

# THE requests LIBRARY

-----------------------------------

Python commonly uses:

requests

For API communication.

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING requests

-----------------------------------

pip install requests

-----------------------------------

# IMPORTING requests

-----------------------------------

import requests

-----------------------------------

# WHY requests IS POPULAR

-----------------------------------

It simplifies:

HTTP communication.

-----------------------------------

# MAKING A GET REQUEST

-----------------------------------

response = requests.get(url)

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE WEATHER API

-----------------------------------

import requests

url = 'https://api.weatherapi.com'

response = requests.get(url)

-----------------------------------

# WHAT response STORES

-----------------------------------

The server response.

-----------------------------------

# CHECKING STATUS CODES

-----------------------------------

print(response.status_code)

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A STATUS CODE?

-----------------------------------

Status codes indicate:

Request success or failure.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON STATUS CODES

-----------------------------------

| Code | Meaning |
|------|----------|
| 200 | Success |
| 404 | Not Found |
| 500 | Server Error |
| 401 | Unauthorized |

-----------------------------------

# WHAT DOES 200 MEAN?

-----------------------------------

The request was:

Successful.

-----------------------------------

# READING API RESPONSES

-----------------------------------

Most APIs return:

JSON data.

-----------------------------------

# CONVERTING RESPONSE TO JSON

-----------------------------------

data = response.json()

-----------------------------------

# WHAT response.json() DOES

-----------------------------------

Converts API responses into:

Python dictionaries.

-----------------------------------

# ACCESSING JSON VALUES

-----------------------------------

print(data['temperature'])

-----------------------------------

# MAKING POST REQUESTS

-----------------------------------

response = requests.post(
    url,
    json=data
)

-----------------------------------

# WHY POST REQUESTS USE JSON

-----------------------------------

JSON is commonly used for:

Sending structured data.

-----------------------------------

# POST REQUEST EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

payload = {
    'message': 'Hello AI'
}

response = requests.post(
    url,
    json=payload
)

-----------------------------------

# HEADERS IN APIS

-----------------------------------

Headers provide:

Extra request information.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON HEADERS

-----------------------------------

• Authorization
• Content-Type
• User-Agent

-----------------------------------

# CONTENT-TYPE HEADER

-----------------------------------

Tells the server:

What type of data is being sent.

-----------------------------------

# JSON CONTENT TYPE

-----------------------------------

headers = {
    'Content-Type': 'application/json'
}

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS AN API KEY?

-----------------------------------

API keys are:

Secret authentication tokens.

-----------------------------------

# WHY API KEYS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

They control:

Access to APIs.

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD API KEY EXAMPLES

-----------------------------------

• OpenAI API key
• Google Maps API key
• Weather API key
• Stripe secret key

-----------------------------------

# SECURITY RISK

-----------------------------------

Never expose:

API keys publicly.

-----------------------------------

# BAD PRACTICE

-----------------------------------

api_key = 'my_secret_key'

-----------------------------------

# WHY THIS IS DANGEROUS

-----------------------------------

Anyone can:

Steal and misuse the key.

-----------------------------------

# USING ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES

-----------------------------------

Store secrets inside:

.env files.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A .env FILE?

-----------------------------------

A .env file stores:

Environment variables.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE .env FILE

-----------------------------------

API_KEY=abcdef12345

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING python-dotenv

-----------------------------------

pip install python-dotenv

-----------------------------------

# LOADING ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES

-----------------------------------

from dotenv import load_dotenv

import os

load_dotenv()

-----------------------------------

# ACCESSING API KEYS

-----------------------------------

api_key = os.getenv('API_KEY')

-----------------------------------

# WHY THIS IS SECURE

-----------------------------------

Secrets remain:

Outside source code.

-----------------------------------

# OPENAI API EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

headers = {
    'Authorization': f'Bearer {api_key}'
}

-----------------------------------

# AI API WORKFLOW

-----------------------------------

User → Python App → AI API → AI Response → Output

-----------------------------------

# CONSUMING EXTERNAL AI APIS

-----------------------------------

AI applications commonly integrate:

• OpenAI APIs
• Hugging Face APIs
• Google AI APIs
• Translation APIs

-----------------------------------

# WEATHER API EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

import requests

url = 'https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather'

params = {
    'q': 'Bangalore',
    'appid': api_key
}

response = requests.get(url, params=params)

data = response.json()

-----------------------------------

# WHY params IS USED

-----------------------------------

params automatically creates:

URL query parameters.

-----------------------------------

# ERROR HANDLING IN APIS

-----------------------------------

APIs may fail due to:

• Invalid keys
• Network issues
• Rate limits
• Wrong URLs

-----------------------------------

# TRY-EXCEPT EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

try:
    response = requests.get(url)

except Exception as e:
    print(e)

-----------------------------------

# RATE LIMITS

-----------------------------------

Many APIs limit:

Number of requests.

-----------------------------------

# WHY RATE LIMITS EXIST

-----------------------------------

To prevent:

Abuse and overload.

-----------------------------------

# API DOCUMENTATION

-----------------------------------

Every API provides:

Documentation.

-----------------------------------

# WHY DOCUMENTATION IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Documentation explains:

• Endpoints
• Parameters
• Authentication
• Response formats

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS AN ENDPOINT?

-----------------------------------

An endpoint is:

A specific API URL.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE ENDPOINT

-----------------------------------

/api/weather

-----------------------------------

# AI APPLICATIONS USING APIS

-----------------------------------

Modern AI systems integrate:

• ChatGPT APIs
• OCR APIs
• Translation APIs
• Speech APIs
• Vision APIs

-----------------------------------

# BUILDING AI ASSISTANTS

-----------------------------------

AI assistants often combine:

Multiple APIs together.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Hardcoding API keys

-----------------------------------

2. Ignoring status codes

-----------------------------------

3. Forgetting error handling

-----------------------------------

4. Using incorrect endpoints

-----------------------------------

5. Not reading API documentation

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Store API keys securely

-----------------------------------

2. Validate API responses

-----------------------------------

3. Handle exceptions properly

-----------------------------------

4. Use environment variables

-----------------------------------

5. Read official API documentation carefully

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What APIs are
• REST API concepts
• HTTP communication
• GET and POST requests
• JSON data handling
• Using the requests library
• Status codes and responses
• API key management
• Environment variables
• Consuming external AI APIs
• API integration workflows

APIs are one of the most critical technologies in modern AI and software development because they allow applications, AI models, cloud systems, and external services to communicate with each other. From ChatGPT integrations to weather systems, payment gateways, recommendation engines, and AI assistants, APIs form the backbone of intelligent interconnected applications.
keyPoints: APIs allow applications to communicate over the internet
REST APIs use HTTP requests for communication
GET requests retrieve data while POST requests send data
JSON is the standard data format for APIs
The requests library simplifies API communication in Python
response.json() converts JSON responses into Python dictionaries
Status codes indicate request success or failure
API keys provide secure authentication
.env files help protect sensitive API keys
External AI services can be integrated using APIs
Error handling improves API reliability
Modern AI systems rely heavily on API integrations
content.practicalTask: Build a Python application that consumes a public weather API using the requests library, processes the JSON response, and displays temperature, humidity, and weather conditions for a selected city.
content.assignment: Create a Python client that integrates with an AI API such as OpenAI or Hugging Face, sends user prompts, processes JSON responses, handles errors properly, and securely manages API keys using environment variables.
content.miniProject: AI Multi-Service Integration System using Python APIs with weather API integration, AI chatbot API support, JSON processing, environment variable management, error handling, and interactive response generation.

days.title: GitHub & Version Control for AI Projects
topics: Deploying to GitHub Pages / Streamlit Cloud

content.theory:
**GitHub & Version Control for AI Projects**

In previous sessions, you learned:

• Machine Learning
• Deep Learning
• NLP
• Computer Vision
• Flask deployment
• Streamlit applications
• APIs and integration

By now, you have built several AI projects.

But one major question remains:

How do professional developers manage code efficiently?

The answer is:

Version Control.

Modern software and AI development heavily depend on:

• Git
• GitHub
• Collaboration workflows
• Deployment platforms

Without version control, developers face problems like:

• Losing code
• Overwriting files
• Poor collaboration
• No project history
• Deployment difficulties

In this session, you will learn:

• What Git is
• What GitHub is
• Version control concepts
• Git commands
• Repositories and commits
• Branching
• README documentation
• requirements.txt
• Deploying AI projects

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

• Manage AI projects professionally
• Push projects to GitHub
• Track code changes
• Collaborate with developers
• Deploy Streamlit applications

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS VERSION CONTROL?

-----------------------------------

Version control is:

A system that tracks code changes over time.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE EXPLANATION

-----------------------------------

Version control allows developers to:

Save, track, and restore code changes.

-----------------------------------

# WHY VERSION CONTROL IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

It helps developers:

• Track project history
• Collaborate safely
• Recover old versions
• Manage updates
• Avoid losing code

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS GIT?

-----------------------------------

Git is:

A distributed version control system.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT DOES DISTRIBUTED MEAN?

-----------------------------------

Every developer has:

A full copy of the project history.

-----------------------------------

# WHO CREATED GIT?

-----------------------------------

Git was created by:

Linus Torvalds.

-----------------------------------

# WHY GIT IS POPULAR

-----------------------------------

Git is:

• Fast
• Reliable
• Powerful
• Industry standard

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS GITHUB?

-----------------------------------

GitHub is:

A cloud platform for hosting Git repositories.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE EXPLANATION

-----------------------------------

GitHub stores:

Projects online.

-----------------------------------

# WHY GITHUB IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

GitHub enables:

• Collaboration
• Project hosting
• Portfolio building
• Open-source contributions
• Deployment workflows

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD USES OF GITHUB

-----------------------------------

Developers use GitHub for:

• AI projects
• Web applications
• Open-source software
• Team collaboration
• Research sharing

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING GIT

-----------------------------------

Download Git from:

https://git-scm.com/

-----------------------------------

# VERIFYING GIT INSTALLATION

-----------------------------------

git --version

-----------------------------------

# CONFIGURING GIT

-----------------------------------

git config --global user.name 'Your Name'

-----------------------------------

# SETTING EMAIL

-----------------------------------

git config --global user.email 'your@email.com'

-----------------------------------

# WHY CONFIGURATION IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Git tracks:

Who made changes.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A REPOSITORY?

-----------------------------------

A repository stores:

Project files and history.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE REPOSITORY EXPLANATION

-----------------------------------

A repository is:

A project folder managed by Git.

-----------------------------------

# INITIALIZING A REPOSITORY

-----------------------------------

git init

-----------------------------------

# WHAT git init DOES

-----------------------------------

Creates:

A local Git repository.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A COMMIT?

-----------------------------------

A commit is:

A saved snapshot of the project.

-----------------------------------

# WHY COMMITS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Commits allow developers to:

Track changes over time.

-----------------------------------

# CHECKING PROJECT STATUS

-----------------------------------

git status

-----------------------------------

# WHAT git status SHOWS

-----------------------------------

It displays:

• Modified files
• Untracked files
• Commit status

-----------------------------------

# ADDING FILES TO GIT

-----------------------------------

git add .

-----------------------------------

# WHAT git add DOES

-----------------------------------

Moves files into:

The staging area.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS THE STAGING AREA?

-----------------------------------

Temporary space before:

Creating a commit.

-----------------------------------

# CREATING A COMMIT

-----------------------------------

git commit -m 'Initial commit'

-----------------------------------

# WHAT -m DOES

-----------------------------------

Adds:

A commit message.

-----------------------------------

# WHY COMMIT MESSAGES MATTER

-----------------------------------

Good commit messages explain:

What changed.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE GOOD COMMIT MESSAGES

-----------------------------------

• Added Flask API

• Fixed prediction bug

• Improved Streamlit dashboard

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS GITHUB REPOSITORY SETUP?

-----------------------------------

A GitHub repository stores:

Projects online.

-----------------------------------

# CREATING A GITHUB REPOSITORY

-----------------------------------

Steps:

1. Login to GitHub

2. Click New Repository

3. Enter repository name

4. Create repository

-----------------------------------

# CONNECTING LOCAL REPOSITORY TO GITHUB

-----------------------------------

git remote add origin repository_url

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A REMOTE?

-----------------------------------

A remote repository exists:

Online.

-----------------------------------

# PUSHING CODE TO GITHUB

-----------------------------------

git push -u origin main

-----------------------------------

# WHAT git push DOES

-----------------------------------

Uploads code to:

GitHub.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS PULLING?

-----------------------------------

git pull

-----------------------------------

# WHAT git pull DOES

-----------------------------------

Downloads latest updates from:

GitHub.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS CLONING?

-----------------------------------

git clone repository_url

-----------------------------------

# WHAT git clone DOES

-----------------------------------

Downloads an entire repository.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS BRANCHING?

-----------------------------------

Branches allow developers to:

Work on features separately.

-----------------------------------

# WHY BRANCHES ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Branches prevent:

Breaking the main project.

-----------------------------------

# CREATING A BRANCH

-----------------------------------

git branch feature-branch

-----------------------------------

# SWITCHING BRANCHES

-----------------------------------

git checkout feature-branch

-----------------------------------

# MODERN SHORTCUT

-----------------------------------

git checkout -b feature-branch

-----------------------------------

# WHAT THIS DOES

-----------------------------------

Creates and switches to:

A new branch.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS MERGING?

-----------------------------------

Merging combines:

Branch changes together.

-----------------------------------

# README.md FILE

-----------------------------------

README.md explains:

The project.

-----------------------------------

# WHY README FILES MATTER

-----------------------------------

README files help users:

Understand projects quickly.

-----------------------------------

# GOOD README CONTENT

-----------------------------------

A good README includes:

• Project title
• Description
• Features
• Installation steps
• Usage instructions
• Screenshots
• Technologies used

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE README STRUCTURE

-----------------------------------

# AI Chatbot

## Features

## Installation

## Usage

## Technologies Used

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS requirements.txt?

-----------------------------------

requirements.txt stores:

Python dependencies.

-----------------------------------

# WHY requirements.txt IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

It allows others to:

Install required packages easily.

-----------------------------------

# GENERATING requirements.txt

-----------------------------------

pip freeze > requirements.txt

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING REQUIREMENTS

-----------------------------------

pip install -r requirements.txt

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS .gitignore?

-----------------------------------

.gitignore excludes:

Unwanted files from Git.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLES OF IGNORED FILES

-----------------------------------

• venv/

• __pycache__/

• .env

• model weights

-----------------------------------

# WHY .env FILES SHOULD NOT BE PUSHED

-----------------------------------

They may contain:

Secret API keys.

-----------------------------------

# AI PROJECT STRUCTURE

-----------------------------------

project/

├── app.py

├── model.pkl

├── requirements.txt

├── README.md

├── .gitignore

└── dataset.csv

-----------------------------------

# GITHUB AS A PORTFOLIO

-----------------------------------

GitHub profiles showcase:

Developer skills and projects.

-----------------------------------

# WHY AI ENGINEERS NEED GITHUB

-----------------------------------

Recruiters often evaluate:

GitHub profiles.

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT CLOUD DEPLOYMENT

-----------------------------------

Streamlit Cloud hosts:

Streamlit applications online.

-----------------------------------

# DEPLOYMENT WORKFLOW

-----------------------------------

1. Push project to GitHub

2. Connect GitHub repository

3. Deploy using Streamlit Cloud

4. Share public app link

-----------------------------------

# WHY DEPLOYMENT MATTERS

-----------------------------------

Deployment makes AI projects:

Accessible worldwide.

-----------------------------------

# GITHUB PAGES

-----------------------------------

GitHub Pages hosts:

Static websites.

-----------------------------------

# AI PROJECTS YOU CAN DEPLOY

-----------------------------------

• Streamlit dashboards
• Portfolio websites
• Documentation sites
• AI demos

-----------------------------------

# OPEN-SOURCE CONTRIBUTIONS

-----------------------------------

GitHub enables developers to:

Contribute to public projects.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A PULL REQUEST?

-----------------------------------

A pull request proposes:

Changes to a repository.

-----------------------------------

# WHY PULL REQUESTS MATTER

-----------------------------------

They enable:

Code review and collaboration.

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Forgetting to commit changes

-----------------------------------

2. Pushing secret API keys

-----------------------------------

3. Poor README documentation

-----------------------------------

4. Ignoring .gitignore

-----------------------------------

5. Using unclear commit messages

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Commit regularly

-----------------------------------

2. Write meaningful commit messages

-----------------------------------

3. Keep repositories organized

-----------------------------------

4. Protect secret keys using .env

-----------------------------------

5. Maintain professional README files

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What version control is
• Git fundamentals
• GitHub repositories
• Git commands and workflows
• Commits and branches
• README documentation
• requirements.txt usage
• .gitignore importance
• Streamlit deployment workflow
• GitHub portfolio building
• AI project collaboration

Git and GitHub are among the most essential tools for modern AI engineers and software developers because they provide professional project management, collaboration, deployment, and portfolio-building capabilities. From Machine Learning systems to large-scale AI platforms, almost every modern software project depends on Git-based workflows for development and deployment.
keyPoints: Git is a distributed version control system
GitHub hosts repositories online for collaboration
Repositories store project files and history
Commits save project snapshots
git add and git commit are core Git commands
Branches help developers work on features safely
README.md documents project information
requirements.txt stores Python dependencies
.gitignore prevents unwanted files from being pushed
GitHub profiles act as developer portfolios
Streamlit Cloud enables easy AI app deployment
Version control is essential for professional AI development
content.practicalTask: Push a complete Machine Learning project to GitHub with a professional README.md file, requirements.txt, proper project structure, and a clean commit history.
content.assignment: Set up a professional GitHub profile and upload at least 3 Machine Learning or AI projects from previous internship sessions. Include proper documentation, screenshots, requirements.txt, and deployment links where applicable.
content.miniProject: Machine Learning Web Application Deployment using Flask and Streamlit with GitHub repository management, version control workflows, professional README documentation, Streamlit Cloud deployment, and REST API integration.

weeks.title: Advanced AI: Hugging Face, LLMs & AI Ethics
weeks.assignment: Fine-tune a Hugging Face transformer model on a custom text classification dataset.
weeks.miniProject: AI Text Summarizer & Q&A System using Hugging Face Transformers.

content.theory:
**Introduction to Hugging Face Transformers**

In previous NLP sessions, you learned:

• Text preprocessing
• Tokenization
• TF-IDF
• Sentiment analysis
• Word embeddings
• Chatbot development

You also learned:

• Neural networks
• Deep Learning
• APIs
• Deployment

Today, you will enter one of the most powerful areas of modern Artificial Intelligence:

Transformers.

Transformers have completely changed the AI industry.

Modern AI systems like:

• ChatGPT
• Gemini
• Claude
• DeepSeek
• Copilot

are all based on:

Transformer architectures.

In this session, you will learn:

• What transformers are
• Why transformers are revolutionary
• BERT and GPT overview
• Hugging Face ecosystem
• Hugging Face Hub
• Using pretrained AI models
• pipeline() API
• Running NLP tasks in a few lines of code

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

• Use pretrained transformer models
• Perform NLP tasks instantly
• Build AI-powered NLP applications
• Use Hugging Face pipelines
• Explore modern AI systems

-----------------------------------

# WHAT ARE TRANSFORMERS?

-----------------------------------

Transformers are:

Deep Learning architectures designed for sequence processing.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE DEFINITION

-----------------------------------

Transformers help AI systems:

Understand language context efficiently.

-----------------------------------

# BEFORE TRANSFORMERS

-----------------------------------

Older NLP systems used:

• RNNs
• LSTMs
• GRUs

-----------------------------------

# PROBLEMS WITH OLDER MODELS

-----------------------------------

Older models struggled with:

• Long-term dependencies
• Parallel processing
• Large-scale training

-----------------------------------

# WHAT MADE TRANSFORMERS REVOLUTIONARY?

-----------------------------------

Transformers introduced:

Attention mechanisms.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS ATTENTION?

-----------------------------------

Attention helps models:

Focus on important words.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Sentence:

'He dropped the glass because it was fragile.'

Transformers understand:

'it' refers to:

glass.

-----------------------------------

# WHY CONTEXT MATTERS

-----------------------------------

Language understanding depends heavily on:

Context.

-----------------------------------

# THE TRANSFORMER PAPER

-----------------------------------

Transformers were introduced in:

'Attention Is All You Need'

by Google researchers.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT ARE LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS?

-----------------------------------

LLMs are:

Large transformer-based AI models trained on massive text datasets.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLES OF TRANSFORMER MODELS

-----------------------------------

• BERT
• GPT
• T5
• RoBERTa
• DistilBERT
• LLaMA

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS BERT?

-----------------------------------

BERT stands for:

Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT MAKES BERT SPECIAL?

-----------------------------------

BERT reads:

Text in both directions.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

BERT understands:

Left and right context together.

-----------------------------------

# BERT IS GOOD FOR

-----------------------------------

• Classification
• Question answering
• Named entity recognition
• Sentiment analysis

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS GPT?

-----------------------------------

GPT stands for:

Generative Pre-trained Transformer.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT DOES GPT DO?

-----------------------------------

GPT generates:

Human-like text.

-----------------------------------

# GPT IS GOOD FOR

-----------------------------------

• Text generation
• Chatbots
• Story writing
• Summarization
• AI assistants

-----------------------------------

# BERT VS GPT

-----------------------------------

| BERT | GPT |
|------|------|
| Understanding focused | Generation focused |
| Bidirectional | Autoregressive |
| Encoder-based | Decoder-based |
| Classification tasks | Text generation tasks |

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS HUGGING FACE?

-----------------------------------

Hugging Face is:

An AI company and open-source platform.

-----------------------------------

# WHY HUGGING FACE IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Hugging Face provides:

• Pretrained models
• NLP libraries
• AI datasets
• Deployment tools
• AI community support

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS THE HUGGING FACE HUB?

-----------------------------------

The Hugging Face Hub stores:

Thousands of pretrained AI models.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT YOU CAN FIND ON THE HUB

-----------------------------------

• NLP models
• Vision models
• Speech models
• Datasets
• Model documentation

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A PRETRAINED MODEL?

-----------------------------------

A pretrained model is:

A model already trained on large datasets.

-----------------------------------

# WHY PRETRAINED MODELS ARE POWERFUL

-----------------------------------

Developers can use AI models:

Without training from scratch.

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING TRANSFORMERS

-----------------------------------

pip install transformers

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING TORCH

-----------------------------------

pip install torch

-----------------------------------

# IMPORTING pipeline

-----------------------------------

from transformers import pipeline

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS pipeline()?

-----------------------------------

pipeline() provides:

A simple interface for NLP tasks.

-----------------------------------

# WHY pipeline() IS POPULAR

-----------------------------------

It allows developers to:

Use advanced AI models in a few lines of code.

-----------------------------------

# SENTIMENT ANALYSIS PIPELINE

-----------------------------------

classifier = pipeline('sentiment-analysis')

-----------------------------------

# USING THE PIPELINE

-----------------------------------

result = classifier('I love AI')

print(result)

-----------------------------------

# SAMPLE OUTPUT

-----------------------------------

[
    {
        'label': 'POSITIVE',
        'score': 0.99
    }
]

-----------------------------------

# WHAT THE MODEL DID

-----------------------------------

The transformer model:

Predicted sentiment automatically.

-----------------------------------

# TEXT SUMMARIZATION PIPELINE

-----------------------------------

summarizer = pipeline('summarization')

-----------------------------------

# USING SUMMARIZATION

-----------------------------------

summary = summarizer(long_text)

-----------------------------------

# WHAT SUMMARIZATION DOES

-----------------------------------

It converts:

Long text into shorter summaries.

-----------------------------------

# QUESTION ANSWERING PIPELINE

-----------------------------------

qa = pipeline('question-answering')

-----------------------------------

# QUESTION ANSWERING EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

result = qa(
    question='What is AI?',
    context='Artificial Intelligence is the simulation of human intelligence.'
)

-----------------------------------

# WHAT THE MODEL RETURNS

-----------------------------------

The model extracts:

The answer from context.

-----------------------------------

# TEXT GENERATION PIPELINE

-----------------------------------

generator = pipeline('text-generation')

-----------------------------------

# TEXT GENERATION EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

result = generator(
    'Artificial Intelligence is',
    max_length=30
)

-----------------------------------

# WHAT TEXT GENERATION DOES

-----------------------------------

The model predicts:

Next words intelligently.

-----------------------------------

# TRANSLATION PIPELINE

-----------------------------------

translator = pipeline(
    'translation_en_to_fr'
)

-----------------------------------

# WHAT TRANSLATION MODELS DO

-----------------------------------

Convert text between:

Different languages.

-----------------------------------

# ZERO-SHOT CLASSIFICATION

-----------------------------------

classifier = pipeline(
    'zero-shot-classification'
)

-----------------------------------

# WHY ZERO-SHOT IS POWERFUL

-----------------------------------

Models classify text:

Without task-specific training.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT ARE MODEL CARDS?

-----------------------------------

Model cards describe:

AI model details.

-----------------------------------

# MODEL CARD INFORMATION

-----------------------------------

Model cards include:

• Model purpose
• Training datasets
• Accuracy
• Limitations
• Usage examples

-----------------------------------

# WHY MODEL CARDS MATTER

-----------------------------------

They help developers:

Understand models properly.

-----------------------------------

# USING SPECIFIC MODELS

-----------------------------------

classifier = pipeline(
    'sentiment-analysis',
    model='distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english'
)

-----------------------------------

# WHY MODEL SELECTION MATTERS

-----------------------------------

Different models have:

Different strengths.

-----------------------------------

# GPU VS CPU

-----------------------------------

Transformer models run faster on:

GPUs.

-----------------------------------

# WHY TRANSFORMERS NEED HIGH COMPUTATION

-----------------------------------

Transformer models contain:

Millions or billions of parameters.

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD TRANSFORMER APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

Transformers power:

• ChatGPT
• AI assistants
• Translation systems
• Search engines
• Recommendation systems
• Code generation tools

-----------------------------------

# HUGGING FACE + STREAMLIT

-----------------------------------

Transformers can integrate with:

Streamlit applications.

-----------------------------------

# HUGGING FACE + FLASK

-----------------------------------

Transformers can also be deployed using:

Flask APIs.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE AI APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

You can build:

• AI chatbots
• Text summarizers
• AI writing assistants
• Sentiment analyzers
• AI translators

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Using very large models on weak hardware

-----------------------------------

2. Ignoring model limitations

-----------------------------------

3. Using incorrect pipeline tasks

-----------------------------------

4. Forgetting internet access for downloads

-----------------------------------

5. Not reading model cards

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Start with smaller models

-----------------------------------

2. Read official documentation

-----------------------------------

3. Understand model limitations

-----------------------------------

4. Use pretrained pipelines first

-----------------------------------

5. Experiment with multiple NLP tasks

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What transformers are
• Why transformers revolutionized AI
• Attention mechanisms
• BERT and GPT overview
• Hugging Face ecosystem
• Hugging Face Hub
• Pretrained transformer models
• pipeline() API usage
• Sentiment analysis
• Summarization
• Question answering
• Text generation
• Translation models

Transformers are among the most important breakthroughs in Artificial Intelligence because they enable machines to understand and generate human language at unprecedented levels. Modern AI systems such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and many advanced NLP tools are all powered by transformer architectures. Hugging Face makes these powerful AI technologies accessible to developers through pretrained models and easy-to-use APIs.
keyPoints: Transformers are advanced Deep Learning architectures for NLP
Attention mechanisms help models understand context
BERT focuses on language understanding tasks
GPT focuses on text generation tasks
Hugging Face provides pretrained transformer models
The Hugging Face Hub hosts thousands of AI models
pipeline() simplifies NLP task execution
Transformers can perform sentiment analysis and summarization
Question-answering pipelines extract answers from text
Pretrained models reduce training effort significantly
Transformer models power modern AI assistants
Hugging Face enables rapid AI application development
content.practicalTask: Use Hugging Face pipelines to perform sentiment analysis, text summarization, and question-answering on different text samples. Compare the outputs and document how each transformer task works.
content.assignment: Build an NLP application using at least 2 different Hugging Face pipelines such as sentiment analysis, summarization, translation, or question answering. Create a simple interface using Streamlit or Flask.
content.miniProject: AI NLP Toolkit using Hugging Face Transformers with sentiment analysis, text summarization, question answering, and text generation capabilities integrated into an interactive web application.

content.theory:
**Text Summarization & Question Answering**

In the previous session, you learned:

• Transformers
• BERT and GPT
• Hugging Face pipelines
• Sentiment analysis
• Text generation
• Question answering basics

Today, you will explore two of the most powerful real-world NLP applications:

• Text Summarization
• Question Answering Systems

These technologies are widely used in:

• ChatGPT
• AI assistants
• Search engines
• Research tools
• Customer support systems
• News summarizers
• Educational platforms

Modern transformer models can:

• Read long documents
• Understand context
• Generate summaries
• Answer questions intelligently

In this session, you will learn:

• What text summarization is
• Extractive vs abstractive summarization
• Question answering systems
• T5 and BERT models
• Hugging Face QA pipelines
• ROUGE evaluation metric
• Context window limitations

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

• Build AI summarizers
• Create document QA systems
• Use transformer models for NLP tasks
• Evaluate generated summaries
• Build intelligent AI assistants

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS TEXT SUMMARIZATION?

-----------------------------------

Text summarization is:

The process of shortening text while preserving important information.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE DEFINITION

-----------------------------------

Summarization converts:

Long text into shorter meaningful summaries.

-----------------------------------

# WHY SUMMARIZATION IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

People generate massive amounts of:

Textual information daily.

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD SUMMARIZATION APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

Summarization is used in:

• News apps
• Research paper summaries
• AI assistants
• Meeting summarizers
• YouTube transcript summaries
• Medical document analysis

-----------------------------------

# TYPES OF SUMMARIZATION

-----------------------------------

There are two main types:

• Extractive summarization
• Abstractive summarization

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS EXTRACTIVE SUMMARIZATION?

-----------------------------------

Extractive summarization:

Selects important sentences directly from the original text.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Original document:

'AI is transforming healthcare. Hospitals use AI for diagnosis. AI also helps in medical imaging.'

-----------------------------------

# EXTRACTIVE SUMMARY

-----------------------------------

'AI is transforming healthcare. AI helps in medical imaging.'

-----------------------------------

# CHARACTERISTICS OF EXTRACTIVE METHODS

-----------------------------------

They:

• Copy original sentences
• Preserve original wording
• Are easier to implement

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS ABSTRACTIVE SUMMARIZATION?

-----------------------------------

Abstractive summarization:

Generates new sentences like humans do.

-----------------------------------

# ABSTRACTIVE EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

'AI is improving modern healthcare systems.'

-----------------------------------

# WHY ABSTRACTIVE SUMMARIZATION IS POWERFUL

-----------------------------------

It creates:

Natural human-like summaries.

-----------------------------------

# TRANSFORMERS FOR SUMMARIZATION

-----------------------------------

Modern summarization systems use:

Transformer models.

-----------------------------------

# POPULAR SUMMARIZATION MODELS

-----------------------------------

• T5
• BART
• Pegasus
• GPT models

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS T5?

-----------------------------------

T5 stands for:

Text-To-Text Transfer Transformer.

-----------------------------------

# WHY T5 IS SPECIAL

-----------------------------------

T5 treats every NLP task as:

A text generation problem.

-----------------------------------

# T5 TASK EXAMPLES

-----------------------------------

• Translation
• Summarization
• Question answering
• Text generation

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING TRANSFORMERS

-----------------------------------

pip install transformers

-----------------------------------

# IMPORTING pipeline

-----------------------------------

from transformers import pipeline

-----------------------------------

# CREATING A SUMMARIZATION PIPELINE

-----------------------------------

summarizer = pipeline(
    'summarization'
)

-----------------------------------

# USING A SPECIFIC MODEL

-----------------------------------

summarizer = pipeline(
    'summarization',
    model='t5-small'
)

-----------------------------------

# WHY t5-small IS POPULAR

-----------------------------------

It is:

• Lightweight
• Beginner-friendly
• Fast

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARIZATION EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

text = 'Artificial Intelligence is transforming industries...'

summary = summarizer(
    text,
    max_length=50,
    min_length=20,
    do_sample=False
)

-----------------------------------

# WHAT max_length DOES

-----------------------------------

Limits:

Maximum summary length.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT min_length DOES

-----------------------------------

Ensures:

Minimum summary length.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT do_sample=False DOES

-----------------------------------

Makes output:

More deterministic.

-----------------------------------

# SAMPLE OUTPUT

-----------------------------------

[
    {
        'summary_text': 'AI is transforming industries through automation and intelligence.'
    }
]

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS QUESTION ANSWERING?

-----------------------------------

Question answering systems:

Answer questions from provided text.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE QA EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

Context:

'Python is a programming language developed by Guido van Rossum.'

Question:

'Who developed Python?'

Answer:

'Guido van Rossum'

-----------------------------------

# WHY QA SYSTEMS ARE IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

QA systems power:

• AI assistants
• Search engines
• Educational AI
• Customer support bots

-----------------------------------

# QUESTION ANSWERING PIPELINE

-----------------------------------

qa_pipeline = pipeline(
    'question-answering'
)

-----------------------------------

# QA EXAMPLE

-----------------------------------

result = qa_pipeline(
    question='What is AI?',
    context='Artificial Intelligence is the simulation of human intelligence.'
)

-----------------------------------

# SAMPLE OUTPUT

-----------------------------------

{
    'answer': 'the simulation of human intelligence',
    'score': 0.98
}

-----------------------------------

# WHAT THE MODEL DOES

-----------------------------------

The transformer model:

Finds answers inside the provided context.

-----------------------------------

# BERT FOR QUESTION ANSWERING

-----------------------------------

BERT is widely used for:

Extractive question answering.

-----------------------------------

# WHY BERT WORKS WELL FOR QA

-----------------------------------

BERT understands:

Bidirectional context.

-----------------------------------

# T5 FOR QUESTION ANSWERING

-----------------------------------

T5 can also perform:

Generative question answering.

-----------------------------------

# BERT VS T5 FOR QA

-----------------------------------

| BERT | T5 |
|------|-----|
| Extracts answers | Generates answers |
| Encoder-based | Encoder-decoder |
| Faster | More flexible |

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS A CONTEXT WINDOW?

-----------------------------------

The context window is:

The amount of text a model can process at once.

-----------------------------------

# WHY CONTEXT LIMITS MATTER

-----------------------------------

Transformer models have:

Token limitations.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT ARE TOKENS?

-----------------------------------

Tokens are:

Small pieces of text.

-----------------------------------

# LONG DOCUMENT PROBLEM

-----------------------------------

Very large documents may:

Exceed model limits.

-----------------------------------

# SOLUTIONS FOR LONG DOCUMENTS

-----------------------------------

• Chunking
• Sliding windows
• Long-context models

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS CHUNKING?

-----------------------------------

Chunking divides:

Large text into smaller parts.

-----------------------------------

# EVALUATING SUMMARIZATION

-----------------------------------

Summaries need:

Evaluation metrics.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS ROUGE?

-----------------------------------

ROUGE stands for:

Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation.

-----------------------------------

# WHY ROUGE IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

ROUGE compares:

Generated summaries with reference summaries.

-----------------------------------

# ROUGE MEASURES

-----------------------------------

• Overlapping words
• Sentence similarity
• Information preservation

-----------------------------------

# COMMON ROUGE METRICS

-----------------------------------

• ROUGE-1
• ROUGE-2
• ROUGE-L

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS ROUGE-1?

-----------------------------------

Measures:

Single-word overlap.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS ROUGE-2?

-----------------------------------

Measures:

Two-word sequence overlap.

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS ROUGE-L?

-----------------------------------

Measures:

Longest common subsequence similarity.

-----------------------------------

# INSTALLING EVALUATION LIBRARIES

-----------------------------------

pip install evaluate

-----------------------------------

# USING ROUGE

-----------------------------------

from evaluate import load

rouge = load('rouge')

-----------------------------------

# COMPUTING ROUGE SCORES

-----------------------------------

results = rouge.compute(
    predictions=[generated_summary],
    references=[reference_summary]
)

-----------------------------------

# REAL-WORLD QA APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

Question answering systems are used in:

• Educational tools
• AI tutors
• Legal AI systems
• Healthcare assistants
• Enterprise search systems

-----------------------------------

# BUILDING DOCUMENT QA SYSTEMS

-----------------------------------

Workflow:

Document → Context → User Question → Transformer Model → Answer

-----------------------------------

# STREAMLIT + QA SYSTEMS

-----------------------------------

Question answering can be integrated into:

Interactive web apps.

-----------------------------------

# EXAMPLE AI APPLICATIONS

-----------------------------------

You can build:

• AI research assistants
• PDF question-answering systems
• AI study assistants
• Smart customer support bots

-----------------------------------

# COMMON BEGINNER MISTAKES

-----------------------------------

1. Using very long text inputs

-----------------------------------

2. Ignoring model token limits

-----------------------------------

3. Using weak summarization prompts

-----------------------------------

4. Not cleaning text before processing

-----------------------------------

5. Ignoring model evaluation metrics

-----------------------------------

# BEST PRACTICES

-----------------------------------

1. Use smaller text chunks

-----------------------------------

2. Choose models carefully

-----------------------------------

3. Evaluate summaries using ROUGE

-----------------------------------

4. Clean text before inference

-----------------------------------

5. Test multiple transformer models

-----------------------------------

# SUMMARY

In this session, you learned:

• What text summarization is
• Extractive vs abstractive summarization
• Transformer-based summarization
• T5 summarization models
• Question answering systems
• BERT and T5 for QA
• Hugging Face QA pipelines
• Context windows and token limits
• ROUGE evaluation metrics
• Building intelligent document assistants

Text summarization and question answering are among the most impactful NLP applications in modern Artificial Intelligence because they allow machines to process large amounts of information, generate concise summaries, and answer user questions intelligently. These technologies are heavily used in AI assistants, research tools, customer support systems, and advanced language understanding platforms.
keyPoints: Text summarization shortens documents while preserving important information
Extractive summarization selects existing sentences
Abstractive summarization generates new sentences
Transformer models power modern summarization systems
T5 is a popular transformer model for summarization and QA
Question answering systems extract answers from context
BERT is effective for extractive QA tasks
Context windows limit how much text models can process
Chunking helps process long documents
ROUGE evaluates summary quality
Hugging Face pipelines simplify transformer usage
Summarization and QA systems are widely used in real-world AI applications
content.practicalTask: Use the Hugging Face summarization pipeline with the T5 model to summarize a 500-word news article. Compare summaries generated using different max_length values and analyze the differences.
content.assignment: Build a question-answering system that accepts a document as context and answers user questions using a Hugging Face transformer pipeline. Create a simple UI using Streamlit or Flask.
content.miniProject: AI Document Assistant using Hugging Face Transformers with text summarization, question answering, document chunking, ROUGE evaluation, and interactive web-based user interface support.

content.theory:
**Fine-Tuning Transformer Models**

In previous NLP and Transformer sessions, you learned:

• Transformers
• BERT and GPT
• Hugging Face pipelines
• Text summarization
• Question answering
• Pretrained AI models

Until now, you have mainly used:

Pretrained transformer models.

These models are already trained on:

Massive text datasets.

But in real-world AI systems, developers often need models that understand:

• Specific industries
• Custom datasets
• Specialized vocabulary
• Business-specific tasks

This is where:

Fine-tuning

becomes important.

Fine-tuning allows developers to:

Adapt pretrained models for custom tasks.

In this session, you will learn:

• What fine-tuning is
• Transfer learning concepts
• Dataset preparation
• Tokenizers
• Hugging Face Trainer API
• TrainingArguments
• Model evaluation
• Fine-tuning DistilBERT and BERT

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

• Fine-tune transformer models
• Prepare datasets for NLP training
• Train custom AI models
• Evaluate transformer performance
• Build domain-specific NLP systems

-----------------------------------

# WHAT IS FINE-TUNING?

-----------------------------------

Fine-tuning is:

Training a pretrained model on a smaller task-specific dataset.

-----------------------------------

# SIMPLE DEFINITION

-----------------------------------

Fine-tuning adapts:

A general AI model to a specific task.

-----------------------------------

# WHY FINE-TUNING IS IMPORTANT

-----------------------------------

Pretrained models are:

General-purpose.

-----------------------------------
